A_{b})}_{\text{net back-pressure force}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** The `+P_back·A_disc` term (pushing up) and the `−P_back·A_b` term (pushing down on the bellows top, transmitted to the disc) leave a *net* back-pressure force of `P_back × (A_disc − A_b)`. **Step 2 — Apply the design condition `A_b = A_disc`:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{in} \cdot A_{disc} &= F_{spring} + P_{back} \cdot (A_{disc} - A_{disc}) \\ P_{in} \cdot A_disc &= F_{spring} + 0 \\ \Rightarrow P_{set} &= \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{disc}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** The back-pressure term vanishes completely. **The set pressure is now independent of downstream pressure** — exactly the design goal of a balanced PRV. **Step 3 — Quantify the error if `A_b ≠ A_disc` (partial balance):** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta P &= P_{back} \cdot \dfrac{A_{disc} - A_b}{A_{disc}} = P_{back} \cdot (1 - \alpha), \quad \text{where } \alpha = \dfrac{A_b}{A_{disc}} \end{aligned} $$ For `α = 0.95` (5 % mismatch) and `P_back = 100 psig`: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta P = 100 \times (1 - 0.95) = 5 \text{ psig} \end{aligned} $$ So a 5 % area mismatch still gives a 5 % set-pressure error at 100 psig back pressure. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Orifice Sizing for a Balanced PRV (API 520) For vapour/gas relief through a balanced PRV, the *required effective orifice area* is: $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot \sqrt{P_{set} \cdot M / (Z \cdot T))}} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Where:** - `W` = required mass flow (lb/hr) - `C` = coefficient from API 520 (gas = 356 for `P_back / P_set ≥ 0.5`) - `K_d` = effective coefficient of discharge (typically **0.975** for balanced PRVs vs. **0.951** for conventional) - `K_b` = back-pressure correction factor - `K_c` = combination correction factor (for rupture-disc + PRV combinations) **Step-by-step solution for a typical KTU problem:** **Given:** A reactor contains vapour at `P_set = 50 psig`, temperature `T = 600 °R`, gas constant factor `M/Z = 30`, required relief rate `W = 30 000 lb/hr`. The valve discharges into a header at `P_back = 20 psig`. **Step 1 — Calculate back-pressure ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{P_{back}}{P_{set}} = \dfrac{20 + 14.7}{50 + 14.7} = \dfrac{34.7}{64.7} \approx 0.536 \end{aligned} $$ > Since the ratio exceeds **0.5**, sub-sonic flow equations apply. **Step 2 — Look up `K_b` from API 520 charts:** For a balanced PRV, by definition `K_b = 1.0` (no correction needed). For a conventional PRV, `K_b ≈ 0.93` at this ratio — meaning the *unbalanced* valve would have to be **7 % larger** to pass the same flow. **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \dfrac{30\,000}{356 \times 0.975 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \sqrt{64.7 \times 30 / 600))} \\ &= \dfrac{30\,000}{347.1 \times \sqrt{3.235}} \\ &= \dfrac{30\,000}{347.1 \times 1.7986} \\ &= \dfrac{30\,000}{624.3} \\ &\approx 48.05 \text{ in}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Select standard API orifice size:** API orifice designations nearest to **48 in²** is the **`K` orifice (area = 60 in² for 6K8 size)** or **`L` orifice (96 in²)**. Choose the **`K` orifice** (next standard up). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Tip:** Always round **up** to the next larger standard orifice to avoid undersizing — undersizing a PRV is a major safety violation. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Balanced vs. Conventional PRV | Parameter | Conventional PRV | Bellows-Balanced PRV | Piston-Balanced PRV | |---|---|---|---| | Effect of back pressure on P_set | Direct, linear | Negligible | Negligible | | Process fluid isolation from bonnet | No | **Yes** (bellows seal) | Partial (piston seal) | | Suitable for corrosive / toxic | Limited | **Excellent** | Limited | | Cost | Lowest | Higher (1.5–2×) | Moderate | | K_d (discharge coefficient) | 0.951 | **0.975** | 0.95 | | Temperature limit | ~ 540 °C | ~ 540 °C | ~ 200 °C | | Maintenance complexity | Simple | Bellows fatigue failure mode | Piston seal replacement | | Typical use | Air, steam, non-toxic gas | Toxic, corrosive, multi-valve headers | High-pressure clean gas | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Balanced Pressure Relief Valve Subsystem The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional interaction** of components inside a bellows-balanced PRV, focusing on the *force-cancelling architecture* rather than a literal mechanical drawing (per protocol safeguard). ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Inlet Process Fluid P_in] --> nodeB[Disc Face Area A_disc] nodeB -- Force = P_in x A_disc pushes disc UP --> nodeC[Disc Holder Assembly] nodeC --> nodeD[Spring Chamber] nodeD -- F_spring pushes disc DOWN --> nodeC nodeE[Outlet Back Pressure P_back] -- Acts on TOP of bellows area A_b --> nodeF[Balancing Bellows] nodeF -- Pushes disc DOWN with force P_back x A_b --> nodeC nodeE2[Outlet Back Pressure P_back] -- Acts on UNDERSIDE of disc with force P_back x A_disc --> nodeB nodeF -. Bellows Area = Disc Area A_b = A_disc .-> nodeG[Net Back-Pressure Force = 0] nodeG --> nodeH[Valve Opens ONLY when P_in x A_disc exceeds F_spring] nodeH --> nodeI[Set Pressure Independent of P_back] nodeJ[Vented Tell-Tale Hole] -. Atmosphere Reference .-> nodeF classDef force fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef balanced fill:#A8E6A1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef critical fill:#FF8C8C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px class nodeB,nodeE,nodeE2,nodeD force class nodeF,nodeG,nodeI balanced class nodeH,nodeA critical ``` ## 4.2 Force-Cancellation Logic Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Force 1: P_in x A_disc
Opening Force] --> C[Net Force on Disc] B[Force 2: F_spring
Closing Force] --> C D[Force 3: P_back x A_disc
Acts on disc underside] --> E[Back-Pressure Net Force] F[Force 4: P_back x A_b
Acts on bellows top] --> E E --> C C --> G{Is P_in x A_disc
greater than F_spring?} G -- Yes --> H[VALVE OPENS
Independent of P_back] G -- No --> I[VALVE REMAINS CLOSED] classDef ok fill:#B5EAD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef no fill:#F8B4B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px classDef check fill:#FFE599,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px class H ok class I no class C,G check ``` ## 4.3 Component-to-Function Mapping Table | Component | Function | Force Contributed | Direction | |---|---|---|---| | Disc | Sealing element; lifts to relieve | `P_in × A_disc` | Upward (opening) | | Spring | Set-point reference | `F_spring` | Downward (closing) | | Bellows (top face) | Cancels back pressure effect | `P_back × A_b` | Downward (closing) | | Disc underside | Standard back-pressure load | `P_back × A_disc` | Upward (opening) | | Adjusting screw | Sets spring compression | Modifies `F_spring` | — | | Tell-tale / vent | Detects bellows failure | None | Diagnostic | | Bonnet | Houses spring & stem | None (structural) | — | # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 | CO3, Bloom Level: Understand | 3 Marks]** > **Q:** Define a *balanced pressure relief valve*. State **two advantages** it offers over a conventional PRV. **Model Answer:** > **Definition:** A balanced pressure relief valve is one in which the opening (set) pressure is **essentially unaffected by superimposed back pressure** on the outlet side, achieved through the use of a bellows or piston whose exposed area equals the disc area. **Advantage 1 — Stable Set Pressure:** Back-pressure fluctuations in a common discharge header do not alter the set pressure, eliminating valve "simmer" or premature opening. **Advantage 2 — Process Fluid Isolation:** The bellows seals the spring chamber and stem guides from the process fluid, making the valve suitable for **toxic, corrosive, or polymerising** services. **Advantage 3 (bonus) — Higher K_d:** Balanced PRVs typically have a discharge coefficient of **0.975** versus **0.951** for conventional PRVs, allowing slightly smaller orifice sizes for the same required flow. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | CO3, Bloom Level: Apply | 3 Marks]** > **Q:** A balanced PRV has a disc area of **2.0 in²** and a spring set to give a set pressure of **100 psig**. The back pressure rises to **60 psig** during a downstream blockage. Will the valve open prematurely? Justify your answer using a force-balance calculation. **Model Answer:** For a **balanced** PRV with `A_b = A_disc`, the set pressure is independent of back pressure. Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{set} &= \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{disc}} = 100 \text{ psig} \quad (\text{unchanged}) \end{aligned} $$ **Justification:** The back-pressure force on the bellows (`P_back × A_b = P_back × A_disc`) cancels exactly with the back-pressure force on the disc underside. The net back-pressure force is **zero**, so the valve will **NOT** open prematurely even at 60 psig back pressure. **Answer:** No, the valve will not open prematurely — this is the *defining benefit* of a balanced design. --- --- ## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each module ESE Part B carries one 14-mark question with internal choice (A or B). Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | CO3, Bloom Level: Apply / Analyse | 14 Marks]** > **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **bellows-balanced pressure relief valve**. How does it differ from a piston-balanced PRV? **\[7 Marks\]** > > **(b)** A chemical reactor contains saturated vapour at a set pressure of **75 psig**. The relief rate is **25 000 kg/hr** of gas (molecular weight **M = 44**, compressibility **Z = 0.95**, temperature **T = 350 K**). The discharge header is at **30 psig**. Determine the required orifice area using API 520 for a balanced PRV. Take `C = 356`, `K_d = 0.975`, `K_b = 1.0`. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Construction:** A bellows-balanced PRV consists of: - **Inlet nozzle** carrying process fluid. - **Disc** seated on the nozzle, sealing the valve. - **Stem** connecting the disc to the spring holder. - **Metallic bellows** welded between the bonnet and the disc holder. The *inside* of the bellows is sealed and vented to atmosphere via a tell-tale hole. - **Spring** inside the bonnet, compressed by an adjusting screw. - **Outlet** discharging into the flare/vent header. **Working:** 1. Process pressure `P_in` acts on the disc area `A_disc` pushing it upward. 2. The bellows top is exposed to back pressure `P_back`; the bellows transmits this force **downward** on the disc-stem assembly. 3. Since `A_b = A_disc`, the two back-pressure contributions cancel. 4. The valve opens only when `P_in × A_disc > F_spring`, independent of `P_back`. **Difference from Piston-Balanced PRV:** | Feature | Bellows-Balanced | Piston-Balanced | |---|---|---| | Balancing element | Corrugated metal bellows | Sliding piston with sealing rings | | Process isolation | Complete (zero leakage past bellows) | Partial (piston rings leak slightly) | | Service | Corrosive, toxic, hot gases | Clean, high-pressure gas only | | Temperature limit | Up to ~ 540 °C | Up to ~ 200 °C | | Failure mode | Bellows fatigue | Piston-ring wear | #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** `W = 25 000 kg/hr`, `M = 44`, `Z = 0.95`, `T = 350 K`, `P_set = 75 psig`, `P_back = 30 psig`, `C = 356`, `K_d = 0.975`, `K_b = 1.0`, `K_c = 1.0`. **Step 1 — Convert pressures to psia:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{set,\text{psia}} &= 75 + 14.7 = 89.7 \text{ psia} \\ P_{back,\text{psia}} &= 30 + 14.7 = 44.7 \text{ psia} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Compute back-pressure ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{P_{back}}{P_{set}} = \dfrac{44.7}{89.7} \approx 0.498 \quad (< 0.5, \text{ borderline subsonic}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Apply API 520 sizing formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} A &= \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{set,\text{psia}} \cdot M}{Z \cdot T}}} \\ &= \dfrac{25\,000}{356 \times 0.975 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{89.7 \times 44}{0.95 \times 350}}} \\ &= \dfrac{25\,000}{347.1 \times \sqrt{11.86}} \\ &= \dfrac{25\,000}{347.1 \times 3.444} \\ &= \dfrac{25\,000}{1195.0} \\ &\approx 20.92 \text{ in}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Select API orifice:** Next standard API orifice ≥ 20.92 in² is the **`L` orifice (50 in²)** for `6L6` size, or more closely the **`J` orifice (36 in²)**. Select **`J` orifice** as minimum acceptable size. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > - Forgetting to convert psig → psia (**−1 mark**). > - Forgetting to convert kg/hr → lb/hr if the formula expects lb/hr (**−2 marks**). The formula above uses SI-consistent form; if your formula uses lb/hr, multiply kg/hr by **2.205**. > - Selecting a smaller orifice than calculated (**−2 marks** — safety violation). > - Forgetting to apply `K_b = 1.0` for balanced PRVs and instead interpolating from API chart for conventional valves (**−1 mark**). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model) | CO3, Bloom Level: Analyse / Evaluate | 14 Marks]** > **(a)** A conventional PRV has a disc area of **3.0 in²** and is set to open at **80 psig**. The spring constant is such that the spring force at lift-off is **240 lbf**. The valve is now relocated to a discharge header where the steady back pressure is **25 psig**. Determine (i) the *new* actual opening pressure and (ii) the percentage deviation from the intended set pressure. **\[7 Marks\]** > > **(b)** Explain with calculations how installing a **bellows-balanced modification** (with `A_b = A_disc = 3.0 in²`) on the same valve would eliminate this deviation. Comment on the practical benefit for a refinery flare header system. **\[7 Marks\]** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Given:** `A_disc = 3.0 in²`, `F_spring = 240 lbf`, `P_set,intended = 80 psig`, `P_back = 25 psig`. **Step 1 — Original set pressure verification:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{set,\text{intended}} = \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{disc}} = \dfrac{240}{3.0} = 80 \text{ psig} \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Force balance with back pressure (conventional / unbalanced):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{in} \cdot A_{disc} &= F_{spring} + P_{back} \cdot A_{disc} \\ P_{in} &= \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{disc}} + P_{back} \\ P_{in} &= 80 + 25 = 105 \text{ psig} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** The valve now opens at 105 psig because the *back pressure contributes an extra opening force* of `P_back × A_disc = 25 × 3 = 75 lbf`, effectively reducing the spring's hold-down ability. **Step 3 — Percentage deviation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \% \text{ Deviation} &= \dfrac{105 - 80}{80} \times 100 = 31.25 \,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** The valve opens **31.25 % earlier** than designed — a dangerous condition for low-margin equipment. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **With bellows balancing (`A_b = A_disc`):** **Step 1 — Re-derive force balance:** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{in} \cdot A_{disc} &= F_{spring} + P_{back} \cdot (A_{disc} - A_b) \\ P_{in} \cdot A_disc &= F_{spring} + P_{back} \cdot 0 \\ P_{in} &= \dfrac{F_{spring}}{A_{disc}} = \dfrac{240}{3.0} = 80 \text{ psig} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Setting `A_b = A_disc` makes the term in parentheses zero, eliminating the back-pressure contribution entirely. **Step 2 — Percentage deviation with balanced design:** $$ \begin{aligned} \% \text{ Deviation} = \dfrac{80 - 80}{80} \times 100 = 0 \,\% \end{aligned} $$ **Practical benefit for refinery flare headers:** A refinery flare header typically receives discharge from **5–20 relief valves** simultaneously or in sequence. In a *conventional* design, the back-pressure created by one relieving valve's discharge propagates upstream and **prematurely lifts every other valve** (a phenomenon called **"valve chatter"** or **"simmer"**). With balanced PRVs: - Each valve opens only at its true set pressure. - The flare header can be sized smaller without fear of cross-talk. - Operators gain accurate diagnostics on which vessel is actually relieving. - Reduces false alarms and avoids unnecessary plant shutdowns. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Frequent Mark Loss:** > - Students often write "set pressure changes" instead of "set pressure increases" when back pressure rises in a *conventional* PRV. Be explicit: **conventional PRVs open EARLIER (at lower inlet pressure) when back pressure rises** because back pressure adds to the opening force. > - For a *balanced* PRV, ensure the student clearly distinguishes between **set pressure** (independent of back pressure) and **blowdown / reseat pressure** (which may still depend slightly on back pressure due to friction effects). > - Always quote the design condition `A_b = A_disc` explicitly in the derivation. --- ## TOPIC RECAP — Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaways — Memorise Before ESE:** - ☐ A **balanced PRV** maintains its set pressure independent of superimposed back pressure. - ☐ Achieved via **bellows** (most common) or **piston** balancing element. - ☐ Design condition: **`A_bellows = A_disc`** for full back-pressure cancellation. - ☐ Two main types: **Bellows-balanced** (toxic/corrosive service) and **Piston-balanced** (high-pressure clean gas). - ☐ Conventional PRV force balance: `P_in × A_disc = F_spring + P_back × A_disc`. - ☐ Balanced PRV force balance (full): `P_in × A_disc = F_spring` (back-pressure term vanishes). - ☐ **API 520 sizing formula:** `A = W / (C · K_d · K_b · K_c · √(P_set · M / ZT))`. - ☐ For balanced PRVs, **`K_b = 1.0`**; for conventional PRVs, `K_b < 1.0` (read from chart). - ☐ Standard set-pressure tolerance per API 526: **±3 %**. - ☐ Maximum allowable overpressure (fire case): **10 %** above set pressure. - ☐ Always **round UP** to the next API standard orifice size. - ☐ Bellows also provide **process-fluid isolation** — critical for toxic/corrosive services. - ☐ **Disc-area mismatch** (5 % area error → ~5 % set-pressure drift at high back pressure). - ☐ Always convert **psig → psia** in API 520 calculations. --- > **END OF NOTES — Module 3: Balanced Pressure Relief Valve** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | CO3 aligned | Bloom Levels: Understand → Analyse*

topics.title: Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve** # 🛡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve (POPRV)**, also known as a *pilot-operated safety valve (POSV)* or *pilot relief valve*, is a pressure relief device in which the **opening and closing of the main valve disc is controlled by a smaller auxiliary (pilot) valve** that senses the system pressure. The pilot valve uses the **process fluid itself** (or a portion of it) as the operating medium to create a hydraulic balance across the main disc. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized):** > *"A pressure relief valve in which the discharge of the process fluid through a smaller auxiliary (pilot) valve is used to control the operation (opening/closing) of the main pressure relief valve, thereby providing relief at or near set pressure with minimal valve lift."* > — (Aligned with ASME Section VIII and API 520 terminology)* The main valve remains tightly closed during normal operation because the **dome pressure** (above the disc) is equalized with the inlet pressure via a small bleed orifice. When the inlet pressure reaches the **set pressure**, the pilot opens, depressurizing the dome, and the main valve disc lifts due to the resulting pressure imbalance. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Stage Door" > [!NOTE] > 💡 **Real-World Analogy: The Heavy Factory Gate with a Small Latch** > > Imagine a **massive, heavy factory gate** that opens only when pressure is applied from below. The gate is so heavy that a person cannot lift it directly. However, the gate is held closed by a **small mechanical latch** at the top. When the water level (pressure) rises to a critical level, this **small latch (pilot valve) automatically releases**, allowing the gate to swing open. The gate stays open as long as the water level is high. Once the level drops, the latch engages again, and the gate closes. > > In the same way, the **pilot valve is the latch**, and the **main valve is the gate**. The pilot is sensitive to small pressure changes, and once it triggers, the main valve opens to relieve the system. ## 1.3 Key Distinguishing Features (vs. Conventional Spring-Loaded Valves) | Feature | Spring-Loaded PSV | Pilot Operated PSV | |---|---|---| | Actuation Mechanism | Direct spring force | Hydraulic (pilot) | | Sensitivity to Backpressure | Low (unless balanced bellows used) | High (must be considered in sizing) | | Set Pressure Accuracy | ±3% typical | ±1% or better | | Seat Tightness | Good (metal-to-metal) | Excellent (soft seat option) | | Operating Pressure Ratio | Limited (≤ 90% of set) | Up to 95–98% of set | | Chatter Resistance | Moderate | High | | Cost | Lower | Higher | ## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in POPRV Design > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Standards & Units (KTU 2024 Module 3 highlights):** > - **Set Pressure** $\left(P_{set}\right)$: in **psig** or **barg** (the pressure at which the valve begins to open) > - **Blowdown** $\left(B_d\right)$: typically **2–5%** of set pressure > - **Overpressure** $\left(O_p\right)$: maximum **10%** accumulation above set pressure (per ASME) > - **Reseat Pressure** $\left(P_{reseat}\right) = P_{set} - B_d$ > - **Relieving Pressure** $\left(P_{rel}\right) = P_{set} \times (1 + O_p)$ for non-fire; $1.21 \times P_{set}$ for fire case > - **Orifice Area** $\left(A\right)$: in **in²** or **mm²** (per API 520) # 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition ### 🔒 Stage 1: Normal Operation (Closed Position) 1. System pressure $\left(P_{inlet}\right)$ enters the valve body **below the main disc**. 2. The same pressure is also routed to the **dome chamber above the disc** through a small **bleed orifice** (typically 1–3 mm diameter). 3. The **pilot valve disc is held closed** by an adjustable spring force. 4. **Pressure equilibrium** exists across the main disc: dome pressure = inlet pressure. 5. The disc remains sealed against the seat due to net area effects and any spring assist. > [!NOTE] > **Why doesn't the main disc open in normal operation?** > The hydraulic force on the disc's *inlet side* is exactly balanced by the hydraulic force on the *dome side* plus a small spring force. The pilot keeps the dome "charged" with the same pressure as the inlet, ensuring **zero net opening force**. ### ⚠️ Stage 2: Set Pressure Reached (Pilot Opens) 6. When $P_{inlet} \geq P_{set}$, the **pilot spring force is overcome**, and the pilot disc lifts. 7. Process fluid from the **dome chamber** is vented through the pilot to the **outlet** at a high flow rate. 8. **Dome pressure drops rapidly** below inlet pressure because the pilot flow capacity >> bleed orifice flow capacity. ### 🚨 Stage 3: Main Valve Opens Fully 9. With dome pressure now much lower than inlet pressure, a **net upward hydraulic force** acts on the main disc. 10. The disc lifts, and the **main valve discharges** process fluid to relieve the system. 11. Flow area is established based on the **orifice size** of the main valve. ### 🔁 Stage 4: Reseat (System Recovery) 12. As system pressure falls to **reseat pressure** $\left(P_{reseat}\right) = P_{set} \times \left(1 - B_d/100\right)$, the pilot spring recloses the pilot. 13. The dome chamber is **repressurized** through the bleed orifice. 14. Once dome pressure equals inlet pressure, the main disc reseats, and the cycle is complete. ## 2.2 Force Balance Analysis — The Heart of POPRV Theory ### 2.2.1 Forces on the Main Disc (Closed Position) For a piston-type POPRV with effective inlet area $A_{inlet}$ and disc area $A_{disc}$: $$F_{down} = P_{inlet} \cdot A_{inlet} + F_{spring}$$ $$F_{up} = P_{dome} \cdot A_{disc}$$ **Closure condition** (valve remains closed): $$P_{inlet} \cdot A_{inlet} + F_{spring} \geq P_{dome} \cdot A_{disc}$$ When $P_{dome} \approx P_{inlet}$ (via bleed), this reduces to a stable equilibrium. ### 2.2.2 Forces on the Main Disc (Open Position) When the pilot opens, $P_{dome}$ drops to a low value $P_{dome,open}$ (often near atmospheric or outlet pressure): $$F_{up,net} = P_{inlet} \cdot A_{inlet} - P_{dome,open} \cdot A_{disc} - F_{spring}$$ For full lift, $F_{up,net} > 0$, allowing the main valve to discharge. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Because the POPRV uses the *same process fluid* to actuate, it can handle **much higher pressures and flow capacities** than a spring-loaded valve of the same size, where the spring would be physically impossible to compress by hand at high set pressures. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_c \cdot K_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{rel} \cdot M}{Z \cdot T}}}$ | Required orifice area (API 520 gas/vapor) | in² or mm² | | 2 | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_c \cdot K_v \cdot K_w \cdot \sqrt{\rho_{liq} \cdot \Delta P}}$ | Required orifice area (API 520 liquid) | in² or mm² | | 3 | $P_{rel} = P_{set} \times (1 + O_p)$ | Relieving pressure (10% accumulation) | psig or barg | | 4 | $P_{rel,fire} = 1.21 \times P_{set}$ | Relieving pressure (fire case, ASME) | psig or barg | | 5 | $B_d = \dfrac{P_{set} - P_{reseat}}{P_{set}} \times 100$ | Blowdown percentage | % | | 6 | $P_{reseat} = P_{set} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{B_d}{100}\right)$ | Reseat pressure | psig or barg | | 7 | $K_{p} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{back}}{P_{set}}$ | Capacity correction for backpressure (non-code) | dimensionless | | 8 | $C = 520 \sqrt{k \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\!(k+1)/(k-1)}}$ | Coefficient for gas flow (choked) | dimensionless | | 9 | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho}$ | General fluid flow (Bernoulli-based) | various | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Variable Legend (must memorize for KTU 2024 exams):** > - $W$ = required flow (lb/hr or kg/hr) > - $C$ = gas flow coefficient (function of specific heat ratio $k$) > - $K_d$ = effective coefficient of discharge (0.975 for POPRV per API 520) > - $K_c$ = combination correction factor > - $K_v$ = viscosity correction factor > - $K_w$ = backpressure correction factor (liquid) > - $M$ = molecular weight of gas > - $Z$ = compressibility factor > - $T$ = inlet temperature (°R or K) > - $P_{back}$ = superimposed backpressure > - $\rho$ = fluid density ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of POPRVs in Process Industries > [!NOTE] > **Why POPRVs are Critical in Chemical Process Safety:** > > 1. **High-Pressure Reactors:** Polyethylene reactors operate at 30,000+ psig — spring-loaded valves are impractical. POPRVs (e.g., Anderson Greenwood, Crosby) are mandatory. > 2. **Cryogenic Storage:** LNG tanks require tight sealing at high operating ratios (95%+ of set) — only POPRVs achieve this. > 3. **Compressor Discharge:** Multistage compressors in refineries use POPRVs to prevent surge and overpressure scenarios. > 4. **Sub-sea Wellheads:** HPHT (high-pressure, high-temperature) wellheads use POPRVs where space is constrained. > 5. **Hydrogen Systems:** Refinery hydrocracker units use POPRVs due to their excellent leak-tight performance. # 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Required Orifice Area for Gas/Vapor Relief (API 520) ### Starting Point — Energy Balance on a Nozzle Consider a converging nozzle through which gas expands from inlet stagnation conditions $\left(P_1, T_1\right)$ to outlet conditions $\left(P_2, T_2\right)$. The **mass flow rate** is given by the **Stagnation Enthalpy Method**: $$\dot{m} = A \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_1}{V_1}}$$ Where: - $A$ = discharge area - $C$ = discharge coefficient (function of $k$ for choked flow) - $P_1$ = upstream stagnation pressure - $V_1$ = specific volume at inlet ### Step 1: Convert to Weight Flow (W) Using Ideal Gas Law For an ideal gas: $$P_1 V_1 = \dfrac{R T_1}{M}$$ Rearranging: $$V_1 = \dfrac{R T_1}{M P_1}$$ Substituting: $$\dot{m} = A \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_1 \cdot M \cdot P_1}{R T_1}} = A \cdot C \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{R T_1}}$$ ### Step 2: Convert Mass Flow to Weight Flow Weight flow rate $W$ in lb/hr is related to mass flow by gravity, but for engineering units (lb/hr): $$W = A \cdot C \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{T_1}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{C_{\text{const}}}$$ The API 520 standard consolidates constants into a numerical form: $$W = A \cdot C \cdot K_d \cdot K_c \cdot K_v \cdot P_{rel} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot T_{rel}}}$$ ### Step 3: Solve for the Required Orifice Area Rearranging algebraically to isolate $A$: $$\boxed{A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_c \cdot K_v \cdot P_{rel} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot T_{rel}}}}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Standard API 520 form** also writes this as: > $$A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_c \cdot K_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{rel} \cdot M}{Z \cdot T_{rel}}}}$$ > Both forms are algebraically equivalent — the only difference is whether $P_{rel}$ is inside or outside the square root. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacity Correction Factor for Backpressure ($K_p$) ### Background For non-balanced POPRVs, the presence of **superimposed backpressure** $\left(P_{back}\right)$ in the outlet header reduces the effective relieving capacity. ### Step 1: Define the Pressure Ratio $$y = \dfrac{P_{back}}{P_{set}}$$ Where $y$ is the dimensionless backpressure ratio. ### Step 2: Empirical Correlation (API 520) API 520 provides an empirical correction: $$K_p = 1 - \alpha \cdot y$$ Where $\alpha$ is a constant depending on valve design (typically $\alpha \approx 0.5$ to $1.0$). ### Step 3: Simplified Form Used in Industry For **pilot-operated valves with bellows** (compensated for backpressure): $$K_p = 1 - \dfrac{P_{back}}{P_{set}}$$ ### Step 4: Application in Sizing The required orifice area is **inflated** by the inverse of $K_p$: $$\boxed{A_{corrected} = \dfrac{A_{ideal}}{K_p}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Rule:** If $K_p \leq 0.90$, API 520 mandates a **larger orifice size** to compensate. Failure to apply this correction is a **top-3 cause of undersized relief valves** in process plant audits (per CCPS guidelines). ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Blowdown from First Principles ### Step 1: Define Net Disc Lift Force During Operation During valve opening, the main disc experiences: - **Upward force**: $F_{up} = P_{inlet} \cdot A_{disc}$ - **Downward force**: $F_{down} = P_{dome} \cdot A_{disc} + F_{spring} + F_{weight}$ - **Friction force**: $F_{fric}$ (opposing motion) ### Step 2: Equilibrium at Set Pressure (Just Opening) For the valve to *just* open at $P_{set}$: $$P_{set} \cdot A_{disc} = P_{dome} \cdot A_{disc} + F_{spring} + F_{weight}$$ Solving for the spring force (which is fixed): $$F_{spring} = \left(P_{set} - P_{dome}\right) A_{disc} - F_{weight}$$ ### Step 3: Equilibrium at Reseat Pressure (Just Closing) At reseat, the dome has repressurized to nearly $P_{reseat}$: $$P_{reseat} \cdot A_{disc} = P_{dome,reseat} \cdot A_{disc} + F_{spring} + F_{weight}$$ Assuming $P_{dome,reseat} \approx P_{reseat}$ (when pilot closes): $$P_{reseat} \cdot A_{disc} = P_{reseat} \cdot A_{disc} + F_{spring} + F_{weight}$$ This is **only satisfied** if the **friction force** prevents reseating until pressure drops below $P_{set}$. ### Step 4: Solving for Blowdown Including friction, the reseat occurs when: $$P_{set} \cdot A_{disc} = P_{reseat} \cdot A_{disc} + F_{fric} + F_{spring}$$ Combining with the open-condition equation and eliminating $F_{spring}$: $$P_{set} - P_{reseat} = \dfrac{F_{fric}}{A_{disc}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{B_d = \dfrac{F_{fric}}{P_{set} \cdot A_{disc}} \times 100\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The blowdown is *directly proportional* to disc friction and *inversely proportional* to set pressure. This is why **low-pressure systems** have proportionally **larger blowdowns** than high-pressure systems. ## 3.4 Numerical Example — KTU 2024 Pattern ### Problem A pilot-operated pressure relief valve is to be installed on a compressor discharge. Determine the required orifice area given: - **Fluid:** Natural gas, $M = 18.0$ kg/kmol - **Set pressure:** $P_{set} = 100$ barg - **Relieving pressure (10% overpressure):** $P_{rel} = 110$ barg $= 110 \times 14.5 = 1595$ psia - **Inlet temperature:** $T_{rel} = 350\text{ K} = 630\text{°R}$ - **Compressibility factor:** $Z = 0.95$ - **Required flow:** $W = 50{,}000$ kg/hr - **Specific heat ratio:** $k = 1.30$ ### Solution **Step 1: Compute the gas flow coefficient $C$ for $k = 1.30$:** $$C = 520 \sqrt{k \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\!(k+1)/(k-1)}}$$ $$\dfrac{k+1}{k-1} = \dfrac{2.30}{0.30} = 7.667$$ $$\left(\dfrac{2}{2.30}\right)^{7.667} = (0.8696)^{7.667} = 0.397$$ $$k \times 0.397 = 1.30 \times 0.397 = 0.516$$ $$C = 520 \times \sqrt{0.516} = 520 \times 0.718 = 373.6$$ **Step 2: Take the typical correction factors:** - $K_d = 0.975$ (POPRV) - $K_c = 1.0$ (no rupture disc upstream) - $K_v = 1.0$ (negligible viscosity correction) **Step 3: Compute the square root term:** $$\sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot T}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18.0}{0.95 \times 630}} = \sqrt{0.0301} = 0.1735$$ **Step 4: Apply the orifice area formula:** $$A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot \sqrt{P_{rel} \cdot M / (Z \cdot T)}}$$ $$A = \dfrac{50{,}000}{373.6 \times 0.975 \times 1595 \times 0.1735}$$ $$A = \dfrac{50{,}000}{100{,}663.5} = 0.497 \text{ in}^2$$ **Step 5: Select the next standard orifice size:** Per API 526, the next standard orifice above 0.497 in² is the **"J" orifice (0.785 in²)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** A **"J" orifice POPRV** would be selected. The actual rated capacity at 10% overpressure would then be calculated to confirm it exceeds the required 50,000 kg/hr. # 🏗️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Component-Level Block Architecture of a POPRV The following Mermaid block diagram shows the functional architecture and fluid pathways of a Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve: ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Process Inlet
High Pressure Fluid] --> B[Main Valve Body
Lower Chamber] A --> C[Sense Line to Pilot] B --> D[Main Disc and Seat
Piston Type] E[Dome Chamber
Above Main Disc] --> B C --> F[Pilot Valve Inlet] F --> G[Pilot Spring Assembly] G --> H{Pilot Disc
Opens at Set Pressure} H -->|If Pressure Below Set| I[Pilot Sealed
Dome Charged via Bleed] H -->|If Pressure Exceeds Set| J[Pilot Opens
Dome Vents to Outlet] I --> E J --> K[Vent Line to Outlet] K --> L[Process Outlet
Atmospheric or Flare Header] B --> L E --> M[Bleed Orifice
1 to 3 mm Diameter] M --> I style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style L fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Operating State Machine — Sequence Diagram This diagram shows the **state transitions** during the four operational phases of a POPRV: ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[State 1 - NORMAL
System Pressure less than Set] -->|Pressure Reaches Set| S2[State 2 - PILOT OPENS
Dome Venting Starts] S2 -->|Dome Pressure Drops| S3[State 3 - MAIN VALVE LIFTS
Full Relief Flow] S3 -->|System Pressure Drops to Reseat| S4[State 4 - PILOT CLOSES
Dome Repressurizes] S4 -->|Dome Equals Inlet Pressure| S1 style S1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style S2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style S3 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style S4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Component Interaction Map This block diagram maps the **interactions between subsystems** in a typical POPRV assembly: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph inletGroup [Inlet System] P1[Process Inlet Pipe] end subgraph mainValveGroup [Main Valve Assembly] MV1[Main Valve Body] MV2[Main Disc Piston] MV3[Main Seat] MV4[Dome Chamber Volume] end subgraph pilotGroup [Pilot Valve Assembly] PV1[Pilot Inlet Orifice] PV2[Pilot Spring Chamber] PV3[Pilot Disc] PV4[Pilot Adjusting Screw] PV5[Pilot Outlet to Vent] end subgraph outletGroup [Outlet System] O1[Outlet Header] O2[Flare or Safe Location] end P1 --> MV1 MV1 --> MV2 MV2 --> MV3 MV3 --> O1 MV1 --> PV1 PV1 --> PV2 PV2 --> PV3 PV3 --> PV4 PV3 --> PV5 PV5 --> O1 MV1 --> MV4 MV4 --> PV1 O1 --> O2 style P1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style O2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style PV3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style MV2 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic Reference (Textual Block Diagram) For a more detailed physical layout representation: ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph POPRV [Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve] direction TB T1[Top Outlet Flange
Connects to Vent or Flare] --> V1 V1[Pilot Disc Assembly
Set by Adjusting Screw] --> V2 V2[Pilot Spring
Set Force Equals Set Pressure] --> V3 V3[Pilot Inlet Port
Receives Sense Pressure] V3 --> V4 V4[Pilot Outlet Passage
Vents to Top Outlet] --> T1 V3 --> V5 V5[Main Dome Chamber
Connected to Pilot Outlet] --> V6 V6[Main Disc Piston
Area A disc] --> V7 V7[Main Seat
Seals Main Flow] --> V8 V8[Main Inlet
Process Fluid at P set] --> V9 V9[Inlet Flange
Connects to System] V5 -.Bleed Orifice.-> V3 end style POPRV fill:#F0F8FF,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:3px style T1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style V9 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - The **left side** of each valve is the **inlet** (process side). > - The **right side** is the **outlet** (vent side). > - The **dashed line** indicates the **bleed orifice** — the small passage that keeps the dome charged with inlet pressure during normal operation. > - **Solid arrows** show primary fluid flow paths. # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Part A — Question 1 > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** (3 Marks) > > **Q:** Define a *Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve*. List **two** advantages it has over a conventional spring-loaded pressure relief valve. **Model Answer:** A **Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve (POPRV)** is a pressure relief device in which the opening and closing of the main valve is controlled by a smaller auxiliary valve (the *pilot*) that senses system pressure and uses the process fluid itself as the operating medium to maintain hydraulic balance on the main disc. **Two advantages over spring-loaded valves:** 1. **Higher set-pressure accuracy** (±1% vs. ±3%) and tighter seat sealing, allowing operation at 95–98% of set pressure. 2. **Can handle very high pressures** with smaller physical size, because the main disc is actuated hydraulically (not by a heavy spring). 3. *(Any two from the above; or: "reduced valve chatter", "larger flow capacities for same size".)* --- ### Part A — Question 2 > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** (3 Marks) > > **Q:** Explain the function of the **bleed orifice** in a pilot operated pressure relief valve. What happens if it gets blocked? **Model Answer:** The **bleed orifice** is a small calibrated passage (typically 1–3 mm diameter) that connects the **dome chamber** (above the main disc) to the **inlet** or the **pilot inlet port**. Its functions are: 1. During normal operation, it **repressurizes the dome** with inlet pressure, maintaining a **hydraulic balance** across the main disc so the valve stays closed. 2. After the pilot recloses during reseat, the bleed orifice **slowly recharges** the dome to inlet pressure, ensuring the main disc reseats. **If blocked:** - The dome cannot recharge after an opening event → the main valve may **fail to reseat** and will **leak continuously**, leading to process loss, environmental release, and possible fire hazard. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern) ### Part B — Question A (Internal Choice 1) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Pattern, 14 Marks]** > > **Q (a)** Explain the **working principle of a Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve** with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of the pilot valve and the dome chamber. (7 Marks) > > **Q (b)** A pilot operated PSV is to be sized for a reactor relief scenario. Given: > - Fluid: steam, $M = 18$ kg/kmol, $k = 1.30$ > - Set pressure: $P_{set} = 12$ barg $\approx 174$ psig > - Required relief load: $W = 8{,}000$ kg/hr > - Inlet temperature: $T = 200^\circ$C $= 453$ K $= 815^\circ$R > - $K_d = 0.975$, $K_c = 1.0$, $K_v = 1.0$, $Z = 1.0$ > > Calculate the **required orifice area** and recommend a **standard API 526 orifice designation**. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution to Q (a) — 7 Marks **Working Principle (4 marks):** The POPRV operates in **four distinct stages**: 1. **Normal (Closed) Operation:** The main disc is held against the seat by hydraulic balance. Process fluid entering the valve body is also routed to the **dome chamber** through a small **bleed orifice**, ensuring the dome pressure equals the inlet pressure. The **pilot valve** remains closed because the set pressure has not been reached, and its spring force is sufficient to keep the pilot disc seated. 2. **Pilot Actuation at Set Pressure:** When the inlet pressure reaches the **set pressure** $\left(P_{set}\right)$, the force exerted on the pilot disc overcomes the spring force, and the **pilot valve opens**. 3. **Main Valve Lift:** With the pilot open, the process fluid in the dome chamber is rapidly vented to the outlet. This causes the **dome pressure to drop sharply** below the inlet pressure, breaking the hydraulic balance. A net upward force now acts on the main disc, lifting it and **allowing full relief flow**. 4. **Reseat:** As the system pressure drops to the **reseat pressure** $\left(P_{set} - \text{blowdown}\right)$, the pilot spring recloses the pilot. The bleed orifice gradually repressurizes the dome, restoring hydraulic balance, and the **main disc reseats**. **Role of Pilot and Dome (3 marks):** - **Pilot Valve:** Acts as the **pressure-sensing element**. It is small, sensitive, and uses a calibrated spring to set the opening pressure. It uses the process fluid as the operating medium — no external power is required. - **Dome Chamber:** Acts as the **hydraulic control volume** above the main disc. By maintaining a pressure equal to the inlet during normal operation, it ensures the main disc remains sealed. When the pilot vents the dome, it **breaks the balance** and triggers main valve lift. *Sketch: [Refer to the cross-sectional diagram in Section 4.4]* #### Model Solution to Q (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Compute the gas flow coefficient $C$ for $k = 1.30$:** $$C = 520 \sqrt{k \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\!(k+1)/(k-1)}}$$ $$\left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\!(k+1)/(k-1)} = \left(\dfrac{2}{2.30}\right)^{\!7.667} = (0.8696)^{7.667} \approx 0.397$$ $$C = 520 \times \sqrt{1.30 \times 0.397} = 520 \times \sqrt{0.516} = 520 \times 0.718 = 373.6$$ **Step 2: Compute the relieving pressure:** $$P_{rel} = 1.10 \times P_{set} = 1.10 \times 174 = 191.4 \text{ psig}$$ (Equivalent to $P_{rel} = 174 + 14.7 + 11.0 \times 14.5 = ...$ — use absolute units as required) **Step 3: Compute the square root term:** $$\sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot T}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{18}{1.0 \times 815}} = \sqrt{0.02209} = 0.1486$$ **Step 4: Apply the orifice area formula:** $$A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_{rel} \cdot \sqrt{M / (Z \cdot T)}}$$ $$A = \dfrac{8{,}000}{373.6 \times 0.975 \times 191.4 \times 0.1486}$$ $$A = \dfrac{8{,}000}{10{,}357.0} = 0.7725 \text{ in}^2$$ **Step 5: Select the next standard API 526 orifice:** The next standard orifice above 0.7725 in² is the **"J" orifice (0.785 in²)**. **Answer:** A **"J" orifice POPRV** should be selected. --- ### Part B — Question B (Internal Choice 2 — Alternative) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 3 Pattern, 14 Marks]** > > **Q (a)** Compare **spring-loaded** and **pilot-operated** pressure relief valves based on **any seven** engineering parameters. Mention at least one application where each is preferred. (7 Marks) > > **Q (b)** A propane storage bullet is protected by a pilot operated pressure relief valve. The following data is given: > - Set pressure: $P_{set} = 150$ psig > - Reseat pressure: $P_{reseat} = 142$ psig > - Backpressure (superimposed): $P_{back} = 30$ psig > - Orifice area: $A = 1.287$ in² (API 526 "L" orifice) > > Calculate: (i) Blowdown percentage, (ii) Capacity correction factor $K_p$, (iii) Corrected orifice area. (7 Marks) #### Model Solution to Q (a) — 7 Marks | # | Parameter | Spring-Loaded PSV | Pilot Operated PSV | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Set Pressure Accuracy** | ±3% | ±1% | | 2 | **Operating Ratio** (max working / set) | ~90% | ~95–98% | | 3 | **Seat Tightness** | Good | Excellent (soft seat) | | 4 | **High-Pressure Suitability** | Poor (heavy spring) | Excellent | | 5 | **Backpressure Sensitivity** | Insensitive (balanced) | Sensitive (needs $K_p$) | | 6 | **Valve Chatter** | Moderate | Low | | 7 | **Cost & Complexity** | Lower, simpler | Higher, complex | | 8 | **Maintenance** | Easy | Requires pilot servicing | **Preferred Applications:** - **Spring-Loaded:** Low-to-medium pressure air receivers, steam boilers (small capacity), where simplicity and low cost matter. - **Pilot-Operated:** High-pressure hydrocracker reactors, polyethylene plants, LNG storage, compressor discharge — where set-pressure accuracy and tight shutoff are critical. #### Model Solution to Q (b) — 7 Marks **Step (i): Blowdown percentage:** $$B_d = \dfrac{P_{set} - P_{reseat}}{P_{set}} \times 100 = \dfrac{150 - 142}{150} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{B_d = \dfrac{8}{150} \times 100 = 5.33\%}$$ **Step (ii): Capacity correction factor $K_p$:** Using the API 520 simplified form for pilot-operated valves: $$K_p = 1 - \dfrac{P_{back}}{P_{set}} = 1 - \dfrac{30}{150}$$ $$\boxed{K_p = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80}$$ **Step (iii): Corrected orifice area:** $$A_{corrected} = \dfrac{A_{original}}{K_p} = \dfrac{1.287}{0.80}$$ $$\boxed{A_{corrected} = 1.609 \text{ in}^2}$$ **Engineering Comment:** The corrected area of 1.609 in² exceeds the standard "L" orifice (1.287 in²), so a **"P" or "Q" orifice** must be selected. This is a critical example of why **backpressure correction is mandatory** in POPRV sizing. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU 2024 Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Using Gauge Pressure instead of Absolute** in the gas flow formula — API 520 formulas use **absolute** units (psia, °R). A common error is using psig and °F without conversion, leading to **30–50% numerical errors**. > > 2. **Forgetting the Capacity Correction Factor $K_p$** when sizing POPRVs for systems with backpressure (e.g., valves discharging to a common header). The examiner will deduct **2–3 marks** for this omission. > > 3. **Confusing Set Pressure and Relieving Pressure** in the orifice equation. The correct variable is $P_{rel} = 1.10 \times P_{set}$ for non-fire scenarios. > > 4. **Writing the wrong direction for dome venting** — students often claim the pilot *increases* dome pressure to open the valve. Correctly, the pilot *decreases* dome pressure by venting it to the outlet. > > 5. **Ignoring Z-factor (compressibility)** for high-pressure gases — required for $P > 500$ psig or $T$ near critical. Loss: **1 mark** per error. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve** > > - [ ] A POPRV uses a **pilot valve** to sense pressure and a **dome chamber** to actuate the main disc via **hydraulic balance**. > - [ ] The **bleed orifice** keeps the dome charged with inlet pressure → **closed during normal operation**. > - [ ] At **set pressure**, the pilot opens → **dome vents to outlet** → **main disc lifts** due to pressure imbalance. > - [ ] At **reseat pressure** (set pressure minus blowdown), the pilot closes → **dome recharges** → main disc reseats. > - [ ] POPRVs achieve **higher operating ratios (95–98%)** and **tighter sealing** than spring-loaded PSVs. > - [ ] **API 520 / API 521 / API 526** are the governing standards for sizing and selection. > - [ ] Required orifice area formula: $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_c \cdot K_v \cdot \sqrt{P_{rel} M / (Z T)}}$ > - [ ] **$K_d = 0.975$** is the standard effective discharge coefficient for POPRVs (API 520). > - [ ] **Backpressure correction factor:** $K_p = 1 - P_{back}/P_{set}$ (for bellows-compensated POPRVs). > - [ ] **Blowdown** for POPRVs is typically **2–5%** (lower than spring-loaded valves at 5–10%). > - [ ] **Fire case:** $P_{rel} = 1.21 \times P_{set}$ (per ASME). > - [ ] **Standard API 526 orifice designations** (ascending): D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T. > - [ ] **Failure mode of POPRV on pilot failure:** If the pilot fails *open* → continuous leak (loss of containment). If pilot fails *closed* → no relief (overpressure risk). Both are critical safety issues. > - [ ] **Blockage of the bleed orifice** is a **silent failure** — it may not be detected until a relief event occurs.

topics.title: Temperature Activated Relief Valve System, Rupture Disc
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Temperature Activated Relief Valve System, Rupture Disc

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Temperature Activated Relief Valve System (Thermal Relief Valve)

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Temperature Activated Relief Valve** (also known as a **Thermal Relief Valve** or **Thermal Expansion Relief Valve**) is a self-actuating pressure relief device designed to protect process equipment and piping from dangerous pressure increases caused solely by **thermal expansion of trapped (blocked-in) liquids**. Unlike conventional pressure relief valves that respond to a primary upstream process pressure, the thermal relief valve is specifically designed to open when the **thermal energy input to a confined liquid causes its volumetric expansion**, thereby generating excessive hydrostatic pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Per API 520 and API 521 (referenced in KTU OESFT616 Module 3), thermal relief valves are *passive* safety devices installed in *liquid-full* systems that are normally isolated between two shut-off valves (blocked-in condition) and exposed to ambient heat sources such as sunlight, steam tracing, or process heat exchangers.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Imagine a **sealed water bottle left in a car on a hot summer day**. As the temperature inside rises:
- The water molecules gain kinetic energy and want to expand
- Because the bottle is rigid and sealed, the water cannot expand freely
- Pressure inside rises dramatically — sometimes enough to **rupture the bottle explosively**

A **thermal relief valve** acts like a **small pressure-relief hatch** on that bottle. It senses the over-pressure caused purely by heating (not by pumping) and quietly vents a small amount of fluid to keep the system safe. It is the *thermal guardian* of any liquid-filled, isolated equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric used in KTU evaluations:** Volumetric thermal expansion coefficient of water $\beta \approx 0.000214 \text{ per } ^\circ\text{C}$ at $20^\circ\text{C}$. This constant is frequently tested in numerical problems.

---

## 1.2 Rupture Disc (RD)

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Rupture Disc Device** (also called a **bursting disc** or **safety disc**) is a **non-reclosing** pressure relief device that functions by the **bursting (rupturing) of a pressure-sensitive metallic or non-metallic membrane** when the upstream pressure exceeds a predetermined set value known as the **burst pressure** ($P_b$) or **marked bursting pressure**. Once ruptured, the disc must be physically replaced before the system can be returned to service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 Key Concept:** A rupture disc is a **single-use, fail-safe, fast-acting** device. It has zero leakage under normal conditions (unlike spring-loaded relief valves which may have simmer leakage). It is the **primary choice** when (a) the process fluid is highly corrosive, (b) the pressure rise is extremely rapid (deflagration), or (c) absolute zero-leakage is mandatory (e.g., toxic/carcinogenic services).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

Picture a **pressure cooker safety plug** made of thin aluminum foil. As steam pressure builds inside the cooker:
- The aluminum foil stretches and bulges outward
- At a precisely engineered pressure, the foil **tears open instantly**
- All the pressure is released in one sudden burst
- You must then **install a new foil disc** before using the cooker again

This is exactly how a rupture disc works. It is the **"circuit breaker"** of pressure safety — it sacrifices itself (one-time use) to protect the entire downstream system from catastrophic overpressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Burst pressures are marked at a **specific temperature** (usually **$72^\circ\text{F}$ / $22^\circ\text{C}$**) as per ASME BPVC Section VIII. The actual operating burst pressure varies with temperature (typically **increases** at lower temperatures and **decreases** at higher temperatures due to material property changes).

---

## 1.3 Why These Devices Are Mandatory in Chemical Process Industries

| Hazard Scenario | Device That Protects |
|---|---|
| Liquid trapped between two block valves heated by sun | **Thermal Relief Valve** |
| Runaway exothermic reaction in a reactor | **Rupture Disc** (or Spring-Loaded SRV) |
| Pump dead-heading against closed discharge | **Thermal Relief Valve** (for line) |
| External fire engulfing a vessel | **Rupture Disc / SRV** |
| Heat exchanger tube rupture with cold side blocked-in | **Thermal Relief Valve** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Temperature Activated Relief Valve — Operating Principle

### Operational Logic Steps

1. **Normal Operation:** A section of piping or vessel is filled with liquid and **isolated** between two closed valves (or by a single check valve). No flow occurs. Pressure equals the static head at the high point.
2. **Heat Input:** External heat source (sunlight, steam tracing, adjacent hot equipment, ambient temperature rise) transfers energy into the trapped liquid.
3. **Thermal Expansion:** Liquid volume tends to increase according to:
$$\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$$
where $\beta$ is the volumetric thermal expansion coefficient.
4. **Pressure Build-up:** Since the vessel walls are essentially rigid, this expansion is constrained, causing a **large pressure rise** because liquids are nearly incompressible.
5. **Valve Actuation:** The rising pressure overcomes the spring setting of the thermal relief valve (typically set at the **MAWP** of the equipment, or 10% above normal operating pressure, whichever is higher).
6. **Venting:** A small quantity of liquid is discharged to relieve pressure and prevent equipment damage.
7. **Re-seat:** When pressure drops below the blowdown value, the spring re-seats the valve — it is **reseatable and reusable**.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why liquids?** Compressible fluids (gases) expand by ~1000× for the same temperature rise, but they have much lower $\beta$ values for pressure rise because compressibility absorbs the expansion.
- **Why between block valves?** This is the worst-case scenario — fluid is completely confined.
- **Why thermal-specific?** Standard pressure relief valves are sized for vapor/gas service or large fire scenarios; thermal relief valves are sized for **small liquid flows due to thermal expansion only**.

### Sizing Formula (API 520 / KTU Standard)

The required relief area for thermal expansion is calculated from the rate of heat input $Q$ (BTU/hr) into the trapped fluid:

$$A = \frac{Q}{K \cdot \sqrt{(P_{set} - P_{back}) \cdot \rho}}$$

where:
- $A$ = required orifice area (sq. in.)
- $Q$ = heat input rate (BTU/hr)
- $K$ = discharge coefficient (typical value $\approx 0.85$ for liquids)
- $P_{set}$ = set pressure (psia)
- $P_{back}$ = back pressure (psia)
- $\rho$ = density of fluid (lb/ft³)

---

## 2.2 Rupture Disc — Operating Principle

### Operational Logic Steps

1. **Installation:** A thin, pre-stressed metal or graphite disc is clamped between flange holders (the **disc holder assembly**).
2. **Normal Operation:** The disc holds full system pressure with negligible leakage (< $10^{-6}$ sccs for metal discs).
3. **Overpressure Event:** Upstream pressure rises above the marked burst pressure $P_b$.
4. **Disc Deformation:** The disc plastically deforms, domes further, and **fractures along engineered score lines** (for scored discs) or **tears at the designed thin region** (for conventional discs).
5. **Full-Bore Discharge:** The disc opens **fully and instantaneously**, providing 100% of the flow area (no popping, no two-stage action).
6. **System Shutdown:** Pressure drops to atmosphere, process must be stopped, disc is replaced, holder re-gasketed, system re-pressurized.

### Types of Rupture Discs (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Conventional (Forward-Acting)** | Domed toward pressure; bursts when pressure exceeds dome limit | General service |
| **Reverse-Acting (Reverse-Buckling)** | Domed away from pressure; buckles and inverts when $P_b$ is reached | High pressure, vacuum, pulsating service |
| **Composite Disc** | Metal + non-metal (PTFE) laminate | Corrosive service |
| **Graphite Disc** | Impervious graphite | Highly corrosive, high-temp acid service |
| **Scored Disc** | Has engineered laser score lines for clean tear | Two-phase flow, isolating pressure differentials |
| **Solid Metal Disc** | Single piece of metal (Inconel, Monel, SS316) | Cryogenic, high-pressure hydrogen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** *"Differentiate between conventional and reverse-buckling rupture discs."* This is a **3-mark standard question** in KTU exams.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ | $V_0$: initial volume (m³); $\beta$: vol. expansion coeff. (1/°C); $\Delta T$: temp. rise (°C) | Thermal expansion of trapped liquid |
| 2 | $W = \frac{V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T}{\text{specific vol.}}$ | Mass to be relieved (kg) | Mass-based relief sizing |
| 3 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{C_o \cdot K_d \cdot K_v \cdot K_w \cdot K_c \cdot \sqrt{(P_{set}-P_{back}) \cdot \rho}}$ | API 520 standard orifice area | Universal relief valve sizing |
| 4 | $P_b = P_{marked} \cdot f_T$ | $P_{marked}$: stamped burst pressure; $f_T$: temp. correction factor | Actual burst at operating T |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{relieved} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \cdot \Delta P}$ | Orifice mass flow equation | Liquid relief flow rate |
| 6 | $P_{max allowable} = P_{design} \cdot 1.10$ | 10% overpressure allowance | API 520/ASME UG-125 |
| 7 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{P_b \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 1.2 P_b}$ | Minimum disc thickness | Rupture disc mechanical design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to Memorize (KTU High-Yield):**
> - Maximum allowable overpressure for non-fire exposure = **$P_{set} \times 1.10$** (10%)
> - Maximum allowable overpressure for fire case = **$P_{set} \times 1.21$** (21%)
> - Water: $\beta_w \approx 0.000214 \text{ per } ^\circ\text{C}$
> - Typical discharge coefficient $K_d \approx 0.85$ for liquids

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Orifice Area for Thermal Relief Valve (KTU Board Standard)

This is the **most commonly asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU Module 3 examinations.

### Problem Setup

A horizontal cylindrical storage tank of volume $V_0$ contains a hydrocarbon liquid. The tank is isolated between two block valves. The maximum ambient temperature rise expected is $\Delta T$. Determine the required relief area of the thermal relief valve.

### Step 1 — Define the Governing Physics

When the trapped liquid is heated by $\Delta T$, it wishes to expand by $\Delta V$:

$$\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$$

Since the vessel is rigid, this expansion must be **relieved as liquid discharge**. The mass of liquid to be relieved is:

$$m_{relieved} = \rho \cdot \Delta V = \rho \cdot V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Orifice Discharge Equation

The rate of mass discharge through the relief valve orifice follows the standard liquid orifice equation:

$$\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \cdot \Delta P}$$

where $\Delta P = P_{set} - P_{back}$ is the differential pressure driving the flow.

### Step 3 — Define the Time Frame

The total mass that must be relieved equals the rate times the allowable relief time $t$:

$$m_{relieved} = \dot{m} \cdot t$$

Equating:

$$\rho \cdot V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \cdot \Delta P} \cdot t$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Required Area $A$

$$\boxed{A = \frac{V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot \sqrt{\rho}}{C_d \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P}}}$$

### Step 5 — Substituting Numerical Values (Sample KTU Board Problem)

**Given:**
- $V_0 = 50 \text{ m}^3$
- $\beta = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per } ^\circ\text{C}$
- $\Delta T = 50 ^\circ\text{C}$
- $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $C_d = 0.62$
- $t = 3600 \text{ s}$ (1 hour)
- $\Delta P = (P_{set} - P_{back}) = 200{,}000 \text{ Pa} - 101{,}325 \text{ Pa} = 98{,}675 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 5a — Compute the Numerator:**

$$V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T = 50 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times 50 = 2.5 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5b — Multiply by $\sqrt{\rho}$:**

$$\sqrt{\rho} = \sqrt{850} = 29.15 \text{ kg}^{0.5} / \text{m}^{1.5}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.5 \times 29.15 = 72.88$$

**Step 5c — Compute the Denominator:**

$$C_d \cdot t = 0.62 \times 3600 = 2232$$

$$\sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P} = \sqrt{2 \times 98{,}675} = \sqrt{197{,}350} = 444.24 \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 2232 \times 444.24 = 991{,}463$$

**Step 5d — Final Calculation:**

$$A = \frac{72.88}{991{,}463} = 7.35 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

Converting to standard valve units:

$$A = 7.35 \times 10^{-5} \times 10{,}763.9 = 0.791 \text{ in}^2$$

The required relief area is approximately **$0.79 \text{ in}^2$**, which corresponds to a standard **D-orifice (0.110 in²)** × 8 equivalent — but more practically, a single **E-orifice** size is selected from API 526.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Burst Pressure of a Rupture Disc

For a **flat, clamped, circular rupture disc** of diameter $D$ and thickness $t$, clamped at the periphery:

### Step 1 — Membrane Stress Analysis

The bursting pressure creates a membrane stress $\sigma$ in the disc. For a thin clamped plate under uniform pressure $P_b$:

$$\sigma = \frac{P_b \cdot D}{2 \cdot t \cdot E}$$

where $E$ is the joint efficiency (typically 1.0 for ductile metals).

### Step 2 — Bursting Criterion

The disc bursts when the membrane stress equals the ultimate tensile strength $S_u$ of the disc material:

$$\frac{P_b \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} = S_u$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Minimum Disc Thickness

Rearranging for $t$:

$$\boxed{t_{min} = \frac{P_b \cdot D}{2 \cdot S_u}}$$

### Step 4 — Account for Clamping Stress Concentration

In practice, the ASME correction factor is applied:

$$t_{min} = \frac{P_b \cdot D}{2 \cdot S_u \cdot E - 1.2 P_b}$$

### Step 5 — Sample Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Given:**
- $P_b = 5 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $S_u = 450 \text{ MPa}$ (Inconel 600 at room temperature)
- $E = 1.0$

**Calculation:**

$$t_{min} = \frac{(5 \times 10^5) \cdot (0.1)}{2 \cdot (450 \times 10^6) \cdot 1.0 - 1.2 \cdot (5 \times 10^5)}$$

$$t_{min} = \frac{5 \times 10^4}{9 \times 10^8 - 6 \times 10^5} = \frac{5 \times 10^4}{8.994 \times 10^8}$$

$$t_{min} = 5.56 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = 0.0556 \text{ mm}$$

This is too thin for manufacturing, so a practical disc thickness of **0.2 mm – 0.5 mm** is selected, and the geometry is changed to a **pre-stressed dome** (conventional disc) to achieve the desired burst pressure.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection Decision Logic

| Criteria | Choose Thermal Relief Valve | Choose Rupture Disc |
|---|---|---|
| **Service** | Liquid-only blocked-in lines | Gas, vapor, two-phase, liquid |
| **Reusability** | Reusable (re-seats) | Single-use (must replace) |
| **Leakage** | Slight simmer leakage possible | Essentially zero leakage |
| **Response Speed** | Slower (spring travel) | Instantaneous (< 5 ms) |
| **Cost** | Lower initial | Higher initial + replacement cost |
| **Toxic/Carcinogenic Service** | Not preferred | **Preferred** (zero leakage) |
| **Maintenance Access** | Frequent inspection | Periodic replacement |
| **Set Pressure Accuracy** | $\pm 3\%$ | $\pm 5\%$ (but very stable) |
| **Combined Use** | Can be used below RD in series | RD can be upstream of SRV to isolate it |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Thermal Relief Valve Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trapped Liquid Segment Blocked In] --> B[Heat Source Activated Sun Steam Tracing]
    B --> C[Temperature Rises by Delta T]
    C --> D[Liquid Volumetric Expansion Delta V equals V0 times Beta times Delta T]
    D --> E[Pressure Rises Beyond Set Pressure]
    E --> F{Pressure exceeds spring setting?}
    F -->|No| G[System Remains Isolated Valve Closed]
    F -->|Yes| H[Thermal Relief Valve Opens]
    H --> I[Liquid Vented to Safe Location Drain Vent]
    I --> J[Pressure Drops Below Blowdown]
    J --> K[Spring Re seats Valve Closed]
    K --> L[System Returns to Safe Blocked In State]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rupture Disc Failure Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[New Disc Installed in Holder] --> B[Normal Process Operation Holds Full System Pressure]
    B --> C{Upstream Pressure exceeds Burst Pressure?}
    C -->|No| B
    C -->|Yes| D[Disc Dome Deforms Plastic Strain Increases]
    D --> E[Score Line Initiates Crack OR Thin Region Fails]
    E --> F[Fracture Propagates Full Diameter]
    F --> G[Disc Tears Open Full Bore Area Available]
    G --> H[Pressure Discharges to Atmosphere or Safe Location]
    H --> I[Process Shutdown Required]
    I --> J[Depressurize and Purge System]
    J --> K[Remove Holder and Replace Disc]
    K --> L[Reinstall with New Gaskets]
    L --> A
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Combined RD plus SRV Safety Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Vessel or Piping] --> B[Rupture Disc RD Installed at Nozzle]
    B --> C[Pressure Gauge and Tee Connection]
    C --> D[Disc Holder Flanged Assembly]
    D --> E[Space Between RD and SRV]
    E --> F[Safety Relief Valve SRV]
    F --> G[Discharge Piping to Safe Location]
    H[Pressure Switch in Intervening Space] --> I[Alarm to Operator If Leakage Detected]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Explanation of Architecture Above:**
> - The **rupture disc** sits closest to the process, providing primary isolation (zero leakage during normal operation).
> - The **SRV** sits downstream of the disc, providing reusable, settable pressure relief.
> - The **intervening space** is monitored by a pressure switch — any leakage through the disc triggers an alarm BEFORE the disc fails, allowing planned shutdown.
> - This is the **industry-preferred configuration** for toxic, carcinogenic, or environmentally hazardous services (per API 520).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: Define a Temperature Activated Relief Valve. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

A Temperature Activated Relief Valve (Thermal Relief Valve) is a **self-actuating, re-seatable pressure relief device** designed to protect process equipment and piping from overpressure caused by **thermal expansion of a trapped liquid** in a blocked-in or isolated section of the process. It opens when the pressure generated by heating exceeds a pre-set spring value and re-seats once the pressure drops below the blowdown value. It is commonly installed between two block valves, in heat exchanger cold-side piping, and in storage tank lines exposed to solar heating or steam tracing.

**Key elements for full marks (3):**
- (1 mark) Self-actuating / spring-loaded device
- (1 mark) Protects against thermal expansion only
- (1 mark) Installed in blocked-in liquid lines

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Conventional and Reverse-Buckling Rupture Discs. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Conventional (Forward-Acting) | Reverse-Buckling |
|---|---|---|
| **Dome Direction** | Domes **toward** the process pressure | Domes **away** from the process pressure |
| **Failure Mode** | Domed disc **stretches** and tears | Disc **inverts (snaps through)** and shears |
| **Pressure Limit** | Lower (typically < 70% of disc rating) | Higher (can operate up to 90% of $P_b$) |
| **Operating Ratio** | Max 70% of $P_b$ | Max 90% of $P_b$ |
| **Fragment Risk** | May produce small fragments | Non-fragmenting design |
| **Vacuum Service** | Not suitable | **Suitable** (with vacuum support) |

**For 3 marks:** State 3 of the above differentiating points clearly.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

#### (a) A horizontal cylindrical pipe section of volume 12 m³ contains a hydrocarbon liquid ($\beta = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per } ^\circ\text{C}$, $\rho = 780 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The pipe is isolated between two block valves. Calculate the volume of liquid that must be relieved if the temperature rises by $40^\circ\text{C}$. What is the minimum orifice area required if the relief valve set pressure is 4 bar(g) and back pressure is 1 bar(g)? Take $C_d = 0.62$ and allowable relief time = 1 hour. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate expansion volume $\Delta V$:**

$$\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\Delta V = 12 \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 40 = 0.576 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate mass to be relieved:**

$$m = \rho \cdot \Delta V = 780 \times 0.576 = 449.28 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Calculate required orifice area:**

Differential pressure: $\Delta P = (4 - 1) \times 10^5 = 3 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

Mass flow rate needed:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{m}{t} = \frac{449.28}{3600} = 0.1248 \text{ kg/s}$$

Apply orifice equation:

$$A = \frac{\dot{m}}{C_d \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}}$$

$$A = \frac{0.1248}{0.62 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 780 \times 3 \times 10^5}}$$

$$A = \frac{0.1248}{0.62 \cdot \sqrt{4.68 \times 10^8}}$$

$$A = \frac{0.1248}{0.62 \cdot 21{,}633} = \frac{0.1248}{13{,}412} = 9.30 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A = 9.30 \times 10^{-6} \times 10{,}763.9 = 0.10 \text{ in}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $\Delta V = 0.576 \text{ m}^3$, $A_{min} = 0.10 \text{ in}^2$ (select next standard API orifice size).

---

#### (b) Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of a Reverse-Buckling Rupture Disc. List any **four advantages** it has over the conventional type. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A reverse-buckling rupture disc is a thin, pre-bulged metal disc held in a holder. The dome is oriented **away from the process pressure** (i.e., the convex side faces away from the inlet). The disc is held between flanges using a holder ring with sharp upstream-side cutting knives.

**Working:**
1. Under normal pressure, the disc remains in its pre-bulged (reverse) position.
2. When upstream pressure approaches the **rated burst pressure**, the disc becomes unstable.
3. The disc **suddenly snaps through (inverts)** in a buckled mode.
4. The cutting knives shear the disc at its periphery, producing a clean, full-bore opening with no fragments entering the downstream pipe.
5. The disc continues to operate as a pressure-relieving device — the larger the overpressure, the faster and more complete the inversion.

**Four Advantages over Conventional Type:**

1. **Higher Operating Ratio** — Can operate continuously at up to 90% of $P_b$ (vs. 70% for conventional), reducing the need for frequent disc replacement.

2. **Non-Fragmenting Design** — Cutting knives shear the disc cleanly; no metal fragments enter the process or downstream piping.

3. **Better Resistance to Pulsation/Fatigue** — The reverse-buckling geometry resists fatigue failure in cyclic pressure services (e.g., reciprocating compressor discharge).

4. **Suitable for Vacuum Service** — When designed with vacuum supports, the reverse-buckling disc can handle full vacuum without damage, unlike the conventional type which would collapse.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Choice – KTU Pattern]

#### (a) Discuss the various **types of rupture discs** based on their construction and application. State the API 520 recommendation for **combining a rupture disc with a safety relief valve**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Types of Rupture Discs:**

1. **Conventional (Forward-Acting) Disc:**
   - Pre-domed disc with dome facing the process pressure.
   - Stretches and bursts when pressure exceeds dome rating.
   - Used for general services where cost is a concern.

2. **Reverse-Buckling Disc:**
   - Dome faces away from pressure; snaps through at $P_b$.
   - Non-fragmenting, suitable for high pressure and vacuum.

3. **Composite Disc:**
   - Laminated construction with metal + PTFE or graphite facing.
   - Used for corrosive chemicals (acids, bromine, chlorine).

4. **Graphite Disc:**
   - Impervious graphite membrane.
   - Used for highly corrosive service (oleum, hot sulfuric acid).

5. **Scored Disc (Lasered):**
   - Has laser-cut score lines for predictable tear pattern.
   - Used in two-phase flow or for low-pressure large-diameter applications.

6. **Solid Metal Disc (Single Piece):**
   - Single-piece forged metal (Inconel, Monel, Hastelloy).
   - Used for cryogenic and high-pressure hydrogen service.

**API 520 Recommendation for RD + SRV Combination:**
- The rupture disc is installed **on the process side** (upstream).
- The safety relief valve is installed **downstream** of the disc.
- A **pressure switch** is installed in the space between the two devices.
- The rupture disc isolates the SRV from process fluids (corrosion, plugging, polymerization).
- The pressure switch alarms if any leakage occurs through the disc, allowing planned shutdown **before** catastrophic disc failure.
- Combined capacity of the system equals the larger of: 100% of SRV alone or RD alone.

---

#### (b) A 20 m³ tank contains benzene ($\beta = 1.24 \times 10^{-3} \text{ per } ^\circ\text{C}$, $\rho = 879 \text{ kg/m}^3$) trapped between two valves. If ambient temperature can rise by $60^\circ\text{C}$ during a summer day, calculate: **(i)** the volume expansion, **(ii)** the required mass relief, and **(iii)** the minimum orifice diameter of the relief valve if set pressure is 5 bar(g) and back pressure is 1.5 bar(g). Take $C_d = 0.62$, $t = 7200 \text{ s}$. **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**(i) Volume expansion:**

$$\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T = 20 \times (1.24 \times 10^{-3}) \times 60 = 1.488 \text{ m}^3$$

**(ii) Required mass relief:**

$$m = \rho \cdot \Delta V = 879 \times 1.488 = 1307.95 \text{ kg}$$

**(iii) Minimum orifice diameter:**

$$\Delta P = (5 - 1.5) \times 10^5 = 3.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{1307.95}{7200} = 0.1817 \text{ kg/s}$$

$$A = \frac{\dot{m}}{C_d \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}} = \frac{0.1817}{0.62 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 879 \times 3.5 \times 10^5}}$$

$$\sqrt{2 \times 879 \times 3.5 \times 10^5} = \sqrt{6.153 \times 10^8} = 24{,}805 \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$

$$A = \frac{0.1817}{0.62 \times 24{,}805} = \frac{0.1817}{15{,}379} = 1.181 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

Diameter:

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.181 \times 10^{-5}}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{1.504 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.878 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{d_{min} \approx 3.88 \text{ mm} \approx 0.153 \text{ in}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** — Many students mix SI and CGS units (Pa with atm, m³ with ft³). KTU strictly penalizes a factor of 10× error.
>
> 2. **Wrong use of gauge vs absolute pressure** — Set pressure in relief valve sizing is **gauge pressure**, but back pressure must be converted to **absolute** before applying $\Delta P$.
>
> 3. **Using $\beta$ for water in chemical service problems** — Each chemical has its own $\beta$; using water's $\beta = 0.000214$ for benzene, oil, or acid is a guaranteed 0-mark answer.
>
> 4. **Confusing rupture disc set pressure with operating pressure** — The marked burst pressure $P_b$ is at a reference temperature; the actual operating burst differs by the temperature correction factor $f_T$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the "why this device?" discussion** — KTU 2024 scheme awards marks for **reasoning** (Course Outcome CO3: Design/Develop solutions), not just calculation.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Thermal Relief Valve** protects against pressure rise due to **thermal expansion of trapped liquid only** — not for vapor service or fire exposure.
- ✅ A **Rupture Disc** is a **single-use, non-reclosing** device — must be replaced after activation.
- ✅ Key formula: $\Delta V = V_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ — memorize and practice variations.
- ✅ Orifice area formula: $A = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{C_d \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}}$ — appears in nearly every numerical.
- ✅ **Conventional disc** = dome toward pressure; **Reverse-buckling** = dome away from pressure (snap-through action).
- ✅ Reverse-buckling allows operation up to **90% of $P_b$**; conventional only up to **70%**.
- ✅ RD + SRV combination per **API 520** is preferred for toxic/corrosive service.
- ✅ Rupture discs provide **zero leakage**, while SRVs may have **simmer leakage**.
- ✅ Standard overpressure allowances: **10%** (non-fire) and **21%** (fire case).
- ✅ Rupture disc thickness formula: $t_{min} = \dfrac{P_b \cdot D}{2 \cdot S_u \cdot E - 1.2 P_b}$.
- ✅ Always state **set pressure (gauge)** and **back pressure** clearly in KTU numerical answers.
- ✅ Conversion factor to remember: **$1 \text{ m}^2 = 1550 \text{ in}^2$**, **$1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$**, **$1 \text{ BTU/hr} \approx 0.293 \text{ W}$**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rupture Pin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rupture Pin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rupture Pin Device** (also called a *Shear Pin Relief Device* or *Pinned Relief Valve*) is a **non-reclosing pressure relief device** used in low-pressure process systems. It operates on the principle of **mechanical shear failure** of one or more calibrated load-bearing pins. The device remains sealed under normal operating pressure and provides instantaneous, full-bore pressure relief when the system pressure exceeds a pre-engineered setpoint, causing the pin(s) to shear off.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3 – Relief Devices):**
