5. **PPE** (last resort)

**Deliverable:** Corrective Action Plan (CAP) with:
- Action description
- Layer addressed
- Owner
- Target closure date
- Verification method

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Estimating the Depth of Investigation Required

For KTU numerical problems, a useful metric is the **Investigation Depth Score** $S_{why}$.

Consider an incident with $k$ independent immediate causes. For each cause $i$, let $d_i$ be the number of "why" layers successfully traversed. The average depth is:

$$
S_{why} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

An incident in an LPG storage terminal has **3 independent immediate causes** identified by the investigation team. The investigation drill-down depths are $d_1 = 5$, $d_2 = 3$, $d_3 = 4$.

**Calculate the average investigation depth $S_{why}$ and compare to the KTU benchmark.**

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
S_{why} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i = \frac{1}{3}\left(d_1 + d_2 + d_3\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
S_{why} = \frac{1}{3}\left(5 + 3 + 4\right) = \frac{12}{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
S_{why} = 4.0 \;\text{layers}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

$$
S_{why} = 4.0 \geq S_{why}^{benchmark} = 3.0 \quad \therefore \text{investigation is acceptable per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(m)}
$$

However, cause #2 was drilled only 3 layers (stopped at Management System level), meaning organizational/cultural causes for that branch were **not investigated** — a recurring-incident risk remains.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Layer-by-Layer Causes from a Classic Case

**Reference Case:** *BP Texas City Refinery Explosion, March 23, 2005 (CSB Report 2007)*

| Layer | Identified Cause (Texas City) |
|---|---|
| **L1 — Engineering** | Raffinate splitter tower had inadequately sized relief valve; level control failed |
| **L2 — Procedure** | Startup procedure did not require verification of liquid level before heat-up |
| **L3 — Human** | Operator relied on a sight glass that was unreliable; fatigue from 12-hr shifts |
| **L4 — Management** | PSM audit program had been cut; MOC was not followed for a prior modification |
| **L5 — Culture** | Cost-cutting culture; production overrode safety; near-misses were not investigated |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "apply layered investigation to a given incident," use this exact template — one line per layer, with the cause stated as a noun phrase. Examiners award full marks only when all five layers are addressed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Investigation — Onion Diagram (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["LOSS EVENT"] --> B["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL AND ENGINEERING"]
```

---

## 4.3 Swiss Cheese Model — Aligned Holes Causing Loss

```mermaid

    H1C["Hole C"] -.-> S3
    H1D["Hole D"] -.-> S4

    H1A -. ALIGNED PATH .-> L1
    H1B -. ALIGNED PATH .-> L1
    H1C -. ALIGNED PATH .-> L1
    H1D -. ALIGNED PATH .-> L1
    H1E -. ALIGNED PATH .-> L1

```

---

## 4.4 Layered Investigation → Corrective Action Workflow


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Define "Layered Investigations" as applied to chemical process safety. List its five canonical layers.**

**Model Answer:**

Layered Investigations is a structured incident-investigation methodology in chemical process safety that systematically uncovers the chain of causation behind an unwanted event by progressively peeling back multiple causal layers — from the immediate physical failure through human factors, procedural gaps, management systems, and finally to the underlying organizational culture.

The five canonical layers are:

| Layer | Name |
|---|---|
| L1 | Physical / Engineering Layer |
| L2 | Immediate Human / Procedural Layer |
| L3 | Behavioral / Human Factors Layer |
| L4 | Management System Layer |
| L5 | Organizational / Cultural Layer |

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for layers)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Explain the connection between Layered Investigations and James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model.**

**Model Answer:**

James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model describes an organization as having multiple defensive barriers (engineering, administrative, supervisory, management, cultural), each with latent holes (failures). A loss event occurs only when the holes in all barriers momentarily align.

Layered Investigations is the *retrospective application* of this model. Each layer of the investigation corresponds to one "slice" of the Swiss Cheese, and the investigator identifies the *specific hole* (latent failure) in each slice that allowed the loss event to penetrate.

For example, an engineering control (slice 1) failed because a relief valve was undersized (hole), a procedural control (slice 2) failed because the SOP omitted a critical step (hole), and so on. The alignment of these holes is the loss event.

*(3 marks — 2 for Swiss Cheese explanation, 1 for connection)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labeled diagram, describe the **five-layer model** of Layered Investigations in chemical process safety. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how the **Hierarchy of Controls** is applied at each layer when formulating corrective actions, with one example per layer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Five-Layer Model (7 marks):**

The five-layer model of Layered Investigations structures the inquiry into causation from immediate to systemic:

1. **Layer 1 — Physical/Engineering Layer:** Identifies the actual hardware that failed — pumps, valves, instruments, vessels, relief devices. *Example:* corrosion-induced failure of a heat exchanger tube.

2. **Layer 2 — Immediate Human/Procedural Layer:** Identifies what the operator/maintenance staff did or did not do, and whether the SOP was followed. *Example:* valve left open contrary to lockout procedure.

3. **Layer 3 — Behavioral/Human Factors Layer:** Identifies *why* the human acted that way — fatigue, ergonomics, competing priorities, cognitive load. *Example:* 12-hour shift leading to attention lapse.

4. **Layer 4 — Management System Layer:** Identifies which PSM element (out of OSHA's 14 elements) failed — MOC, PHA, Mechanical Integrity, Training. *Example:* MOC procedure not followed for a recent modification.

5. **Layer 5 — Organizational/Cultural Layer:** Identifies cultural drivers — production pressure, cost-cutting, lack of safety KPIs at board level. *Example:* "Just get it running" culture overriding engineering concerns.

*Diagrammatic representation (drawn on answer script):* An inverted pyramid or "onion" diagram showing L1 outermost narrowing to L5 innermost.

*(7 marks — 1 per layer + 2 for diagram coherence)*

---

**(b) Hierarchy of Controls at Each Layer (7 marks):**

The Hierarchy of Controls ranks preventive measures by effectiveness:

| Rank | Control Type | Example per Layer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (best) | **Elimination** | L1: Remove the hazardous chemical entirely; L4: Eliminate the obsolete procedure |
| 2 | **Substitution** | L1: Replace carbon steel with stainless; L2: Substitute a complex manual task with an automated one |
| 3 | **Engineering Controls** | L1: Add a high-integrity pressure interlock (SIS); L3: Improve control room ergonomics |
| 4 | **Administrative Controls** | L2: Revise SOP; L4: Strengthen MOC policy; add shift turnover checklist |
| 5 (last) | **PPE** | L3: Issue SCBA training; L5: Mandate safety culture training for managers |

**Key principle:** Higher-layer (organizational) solutions are preferred for systemic issues; lower-layer (PPE) is acceptable only when nothing higher is feasible.

*(7 marks — 2 for hierarchy explanation, 4 for layer-wise examples, 1 for concluding principle)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper Alternative]**

**(a)** Describe the **step-by-step methodology** of conducting a layered investigation, from scene preservation through to corrective action verification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LPG bullet leak-and-fire incident in a storage terminal has **3 identified immediate causes**, drilled to depths of $d_1 = 4$, $d_2 = 5$, and $d_3 = 2$. Calculate the **average investigation depth $S_{why}$** and comment on whether this meets the OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119(m) benchmark. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Eight-Step Methodology (7 marks):**

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scene preservation — isolate unit, photograph, save DCS data | Initial scene report |
| 2 | Chronological reconstruction — minute-by-minute timeline | Timeline document |
| 3 | Layer 1 — engineering investigation | Failure mode report |
| 4 | Layer 2 — procedural investigation | SOP gap analysis |
| 5 | Layer 3 — human factors investigation | HFAT / TRIPOD-DELTA output |
| 6 | Layer 4 — management system investigation | PSM gap matrix |
| 7 | Layer 5 — organizational/cultural investigation | Cultural findings |
| 8 | Corrective actions + verification | CAP with closure dates |

Each step builds on the previous; skipping any step creates investigation gaps.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per step for the first 6, plus 1 for synthesis)*

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation of $S_{why}$ (7 marks):**

Given:
- $k = 3$ immediate causes
- $d_1 = 4$, $d_2 = 5$, $d_3 = 2$
- OSHA benchmark: $S_{why}^{benchmark} \geq 3$

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
S_{why} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i = \frac{1}{3}(d_1 + d_2 + d_3)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
S_{why} = \frac{1}{3}(4 + 5 + 2) = \frac{11}{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
S_{why} \approx 3.67 \;\text{layers}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to benchmark:**

$$
S_{why} = 3.67 \geq 3.0 \quad \therefore \text{aggregate investigation is acceptable per OSHA}
$$

**Step 5 — Critical analysis of cause #3:**

$$
d_3 = 2 < 3 \quad \therefore \text{cause 3 was NOT drilled to management-system level}
$$

The investigation **failed to uncover root causes** for the third immediate cause, leaving a recurrence risk. **The report should be returned for additional investigation of cause #3.**

*(7 marks — 3 for setup, 2 for calculation, 2 for critical interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Stopping at Layer 1:** Students who list only the equipment failure score a maximum of 4–5 marks out of 14. Examiners expect ALL FIVE layers discussed.
> 2. **Confusing LOPA with Layered Investigation:** LOPA is *prospective* (design-stage); Layered Investigation is *retrospective* (post-incident). Mixing the two confuses the examiner.
> 3. **Using "human error" as a final cause:** Saying "operator error" without drilling further loses 2–3 marks. Always ask "why" at least three more times.
> 4. **Confusing immediate causes with root causes:** "Valve failed" is *immediate*; "MOC was not followed for that valve modification" is *root*. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.
> 5. **Skipping the Hierarchy of Controls:** In corrective action questions, always cite the Hierarchy — Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
> 6. **Units in numerical problems:** Always quote $S_{why}$ in "layers" or "levels," not dimensionless. Examiners deduct for missing units.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Layered Investigations** = systematic peeling back of causation layers from immediate to systemic
- ✅ **Five canonical layers (PHMOC):** Physical → Human → Management → Organization → Culture
- ✅ **Three minimal layers per OSHA 1910.119(m):** Immediate / Root / Systemic
- ✅ **Swiss Cheese Model** = the theoretical foundation; layers = slices; investigation identifies the *holes*
- ✅ **LOPA ↔ Layered Investigation:** LOPA is *prospective* (design); Investigation is *retrospective* (incident)
- ✅ **Why-Tree logic:** each "why" answer becomes the next question
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls** (most → least effective): Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE
- ✅ **Investigation Depth Score:**
$$ S_{why} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} d_i \geq 3 $$
- ✅ **Corrective Action Closure Rate benchmark:** $\eta_{CA} \geq 90\%$
- ✅ **Repeat-incident goal:** $R_{repeat} \to 0$
- ✅ **PSM 14 elements** (OSHA 1910.119) form the Layer 4 audit checklist
- ✅ **Human Factors tools:** HFAT, TRIPOD-DELTA
- ✅ **Cultural tools:** Hearts & Minds, Bradley Curve, DuPont Safety Culture
- ✅ **Always cite** the controlling regulation: OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, EPA RMP, CCPS Guidelines (2003, 2nd ed.)
- ✅ **KTU Mnemonic "PHMOC"** for the five layers — write this on your answer script as a header in long-answer questions
- ✅ **Standard investigation deliverable:** Date / Description / Contributing Factors / Recommendations (with deadlines)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Investigation Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔍 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Investigation Process (KTU 2024 Definition):** The **Investigation Process** in Chemical Process Safety is a systematic, structured, and multidisciplinary methodology employed after an undesired process safety event (incidents, near-misses, or accidents) to identify the immediate (proximal) causes, underlying (root) causes, and systemic contributing factors, with the ultimate goal of generating actionable corrective actions that prevent recurrence.

According to the **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** guidelines referenced in the KTU 2024 OESFT616 syllabus, an investigation is **not a search for blame** but a search for **causal mechanisms and systemic gaps**.

### Key Terminology Anchored to KTU Syllabus

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Incident** | An undesired event that has the potential to cause harm, loss, or disruption. |
| **Near-Miss** | An event that **did not** result in injury/damage but had the potential to do so. |
| **Root Cause** | The fundamental, most basic reason for an undesired event, which, if corrected, would prevent recurrence. |
| **Proximate Cause** | The immediate, observable reason that directly produced the outcome. |
| **Contributing Factor** | A condition or circumstance that, alone, would not cause the event but increased the likelihood. |
| **Bow-Tie Analysis** | A diagrammatic representation linking threats, top event, consequences, and barriers. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Medical Doctor" Approach to Plant Failures** 🩺

Imagine your **plant is a human body**, and a process incident is a **disease symptom** (say, a sudden fever).

- A **bad investigator** would say: *"The patient has a fever. Give him paracetamol."* → Treats the **symptom only**.
- A **good investigator** (KTU standard) would say: *"Why is there a fever? Is it an infection? A metabolic disorder? A side effect of another drug?"* → Searches for the **root cause**.

The Investigation Process is essentially the **"diagnostic methodology"** for industrial systems. You don't just patch the leak — you find out **why the pipe corroded** (poor material selection, inadequate inspection, overpressure, etc.).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "SHEEP-RADAR":** Every KTU investigation must trace the **S**ymptom, **H**uman factor, **E**quipment, **E**nvironment, **P**rocedure — and produce a **R**ecommendation, **A**ction, **D**ocumentation, **A**udit, **R**eview loop.

---

## 1.3 Why Investigate? (KTU High-Yield Point)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 80/20 Rule of Incidents:** It is a globally accepted safety statistic (and a KTU expected point) that for every **1 major incident**, there are approximately **30 minor incidents** and **300 near-misses**. Investigating near-misses is statistically the most efficient way to prevent catastrophic events.

The investigation is performed to:
1. Determine the **sequence of events** (chronology).
2. Identify **root and contributing causes**.
3. Develop **corrective actions** to prevent recurrence.
4. Satisfy **legal and regulatory** obligations (Factories Act, OSHA, EPA).
5. Update **PSM (Process Safety Management)** documentation.
6. Disseminate **lessons learned** across the industry.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

- **OSHA Reportable Incident Rate (IR):** 
$$IR = \frac{\text{Number of recordable injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total hours worked by all employees}}$$

- **Severity Rate (SR):** 
$$SR = \frac{\text{Lost workdays} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total hours worked by all employees}}$$

- **DART Rate (Days Away/Restricted/Transfer):** 
$$DART = \frac{(\text{Days away} + \text{Restricted} + \text{Transfer cases}) \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total hours worked}}$$

> **Bold Threshold Note:** OSHA's **200,000 hours** = 100 full-time employees working 40 hours/week × 50 weeks/year. This is the **standard reference workforce** used as a **denominator normalization constant**.

---

## 1.5 Categories of Incidents (KTU Classification)

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Fire** | Uncontrolled combustion | Pump seal fire |
| **Explosion** | Rapid energy release (VCE, BLEVE) | Propane BLEVE |
| **Toxic Release** | Hazardous chemical emission | Chlorine gas leak |
| **Mechanical Failure** | Equipment rupture | Pressure vessel crack |
| **Process Upset** | Deviation from design conditions | Runaway reaction |
| **Environmental Release** | Spill to air/water/soil | Oil spill |
| **Personal Injury** | Harm to workforce | Fall, burn |

---

## 1.6 KTU 2024 Module 4 Learning Outcomes (Mapped to Bloom's Taxonomy)

| LO Code | Outcome Statement | Bloom Level |
|---|---|---|
| **CO4.1** | Define incident investigation and its objectives. | Remembering |
| **CO4.2** | Explain the stages of an investigation process. | Understanding |
| **CO4.3** | Apply root cause analysis tools (5 Whys, FTA, ETA, Bow-Tie). | Applying |
| **CO4.4** | Analyze witness statements and evidence for causal inference. | Analyzing |
| **CO4.5** | Evaluate corrective actions and lessons learned. | Evaluating |
| **CO4.6** | Design an investigation report framework. | Creating |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Stages of the KTU Investigation Process

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates knowledge of the **Five-Stage Investigation Model** (CCPS-derived).

### 🟢 Stage 1: Initial Response & Scene Control (T+0 to T+2 hours)

**Objective:** Preserve life, secure the scene, and prevent secondary incidents.

**Key Actions:**
- Activate **Emergency Operations Plan (EOP)**.
- Evacuate non-essential personnel.
- Isolate energy sources (LOTO — Lock Out / Tag Out).
- Render first aid and call emergency services.
- **Establish a perimeter** (typically **1.5 × the height of the tallest vessel** for explosion/radiation zones).
- Designate an **Incident Commander (IC)**.
- Begin a **chronological logbook** — every entry is **timestamped** and **signed**.
- Protect evidence (do not disturb debris, samples, log sheets, control system data).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why T+0 to T+2 hours matters:** Studies show **physical evidence deteriorates rapidly** — corrosive residues disperse, electrical lockouts get reset, and witness memories decay at approximately **30% per hour** within the first 24 hours.

---

### 🟢 Stage 2: Evidence Collection & Preservation

**Four Classes of Evidence:**

1. **Physical Evidence**
   - Failed components (pipe, gasket, valve).
   - Pressure indicators, charts, recorder tapes.
   - Sample residues (refrigerated to < 4°C for GC/MS analysis).
   - **Custody Chain** must be maintained — every transfer documented.

2. **Documentary Evidence**
   - Process & Instrument Diagrams (P&IDs).
   - Operating procedures, shift logs, maintenance records.
   - Training records, JSA (Job Safety Analysis), permits.
   - DCS/SCADA data (historian trends — **never deleted**).

3. **Witness Evidence**
   - Operator interviews (first-hand).
   - Bystander accounts.
   - Emergency responders.
   - **Interview protocol:** 5-step structured approach (covered in §2.4).

4. **Environmental Evidence**
   - Weather conditions (wind speed/direction, temperature, humidity).
   - Terrain, drainage pathways, plant layout.

---

### 🟢 Stage 3: Cause Analysis (The Core Stage)

This is the **most heavily tested stage** in KTU 2024 examinations. The investigator uses one or more of the following analytical tools:

| Tool | Application | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **5 Whys (Sakichi Toyoda)** | Sequential questioning for linear causation | High |
| **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Boolean logic for complex causation | Very High |
| **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** | Forward propagation of outcomes | High |
| **Bow-Tie Analysis** | Visual integration of FTA + ETA | Very High |
| **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone / Ishikawa)** | Multi-category factor analysis | Medium |
| **SWIFT (Structured What-If Technique)** | Brainstorming-based risk screening | Medium |
| **MORT (Management Oversight & Risk Tree)** | Organizational/systemic failures | Low-Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Favorite: Bow-Tie Analysis** — A typical KTU question asks students to draw a Bow-Tie diagram for a given scenario. The structure is: **Threats → Top Event → Consequences**, with **Preventive Barriers** on the left and **Mitigative Barriers** on the right.

---

### 🟢 Stage 4: Recommendations & Corrective Actions

Each **root cause** must generate at least one **SMART** corrective action:

> **SMART Criteria:**
> - **S**pecific — Clearly defines what must change.
> - **M**easurable — Has quantifiable success criteria.
> - **A**chievable — Realistic within organizational capacity.
> - **R**elevant — Directly addresses the root cause.
> - **T**ime-bound — Has a definite deadline.

Corrective actions are classified by hierarchy of effectiveness (from **most effective** to **least effective**):

| Rank | Control Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Elimination** | Remove the hazardous substance |
| 2 | **Substitution** | Replace with a less hazardous chemical |
| 3 | **Engineering Control** | Install additional relief valve |
| 4 | **Administrative Control** | Update SOP, add training |
| 5 | **PPE** | Provide respirators (least preferred) |

---

### 🟢 Stage 5: Reporting, Follow-up & Lessons Learned

**Final Report Sections (KTU Expected):**

1. Executive Summary
2. Incident Description
3. Sequence of Events (chronology)
4. Causal Analysis (with diagrams)
5. Root Cause(s) Identified
6. Recommendations (SMART)
7. Implementation Plan
8. Lessons Learned
9. Appendices (evidence, witness statements, calculations)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Distribution Rule:** The final report is distributed to: **Operations, Maintenance, Engineering, Safety, Management, Regulatory bodies (if applicable), and Trade Unions** (for awareness, not approval).

---

## 2.2 Deep Dive: Root Cause Analysis Tools

### 🔵 2.2.1 The 5 Whys Technique

**Origin:** Sakichi Toyoda (Toyota Production System, 1950s).
**Logic:** Repeating "Why?" five times to drill from a symptom to a root cause.

**Example (KTU Typical):**
- **Problem:** Reactor overpressured and ruptured.
- **Why 1?** Temperature rose uncontrollably.
- **Why 2?** Cooling water flow stopped.
- **Why 3?** Cooling water pump failed.
- **Why 4?** Pump bearing seized due to lack of lubrication.
- **Why 5?** Maintenance schedule was not followed due to production pressure.
- **Root Cause:** **Management prioritization of production over preventive maintenance.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The 5 Whys assumes a **linear, single-path causation**, which is rarely the case in complex chemical processes. Examiners expect students to **acknowledge this limitation** and recommend supplementary tools (FTA, Bow-Tie) for multi-path scenarios.

---

### 🔵 2.2.2 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — Mathematical Rigor

FTA uses **Boolean algebra** to compute the probability of a Top Event from basic component failure probabilities.

**Symbols (KTU Mandatory):**

| Symbol | Gate | Boolean Logic |
|---|---|---|
| ⬭ Rectangle | Intermediate / Top Event | Event |
| ⭕ Circle | Basic Event | Independent failure |
| ▭ Diamond | Undeveloped Event | Insufficient data |
| ⊕ Oval (with plus) | OR Gate | $A \cup B$ |
| ⊕ Shield (with dot) | AND Gate | $A \cap B$ |

**Minimal Cut Set:** The smallest combination of basic events that, if all occur, cause the Top Event.

**Top Event Probability via OR Gate (rare events, mutual exclusivity assumed):**

$$P_{TE} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)$$

**Top Event Probability via AND Gate (independent events):**

$$P_{TE} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$

**Example:**
- Basic Event A: Failure probability = 0.01
- Basic Event B: Failure probability = 0.02
- **OR Gate:** $P_{TE} = 1 - (1 - 0.01)(1 - 0.02) = 1 - 0.99 \times 0.98 = 0.0298$
- **AND Gate:** $P_{TE} = 0.01 \times 0.02 = 0.0002$

---

### 🔵 2.2.3 Event Tree Analysis (ETA) — Forward Propagation

**Logic:** From an initiating event, propagate through **safety function successes/failures** to determine all possible outcomes and their probabilities.

**Key Formulas:**

$$P(\text{Outcome}_j) = P(\text{Initiating Event}) \times \prod_{k=1}^{m} P(\text{Function}_k)$$

**Example (KTU Standard):**
- Initiating Event: Pipe rupture (P = 0.001/year)
- Safety Function 1: Isolation valve closes (P_success = 0.99, P_failure = 0.01)
- Safety Function 2: Relief valve opens (P_success = 0.95, P_failure = 0.05)

**Outcomes:**
- Outcome 1 (both work): $0.001 \times 0.99 \times 0.95 = 9.405 \times 10^{-4}$
- Outcome 2 (valve works, relief fails): $0.001 \times 0.99 \times 0.05 = 4.95 \times 10^{-5}$
- Outcome 3 (valve fails, relief works): $0.001 \times 0.01 \times 0.95 = 9.5 \times 10^{-6}$
- Outcome 4 (both fail): $0.001 \times 0.01 \times 0.05 = 5 \times 10^{-7}$

**Verification (sum of all outcomes = P_initiating):**
$$9.405 \times 10^{-4} + 4.95 \times 10^{-5} + 9.5 \times 10^{-6} + 5 \times 10^{-7} = 0.001 \checkmark$$

---

### 🔵 2.2.4 Bow-Tie Analysis (KTU High-Yield Visual Tool)

**Structure:**

| Left Side (Fault Tree) | Center (Top Event) | Right Side (Event Tree) |
|---|---|---|
| **Threats** (causes) | **Loss of Containment** (Top Event) | **Consequences** (outcomes) |
| Threat 1: Pump failure | | Consequence 1: Pool fire |
| Threat 2: Corrosion | | Consequence 2: Vapor cloud explosion |
| Threat 3: Human error | | Consequence 3: Toxic exposure |
| **Preventive Barriers** | | **Mitigative Barriers** |
| Barrier 1: Routine inspection | | Barrier 1: Firewater deluge |
| Barrier 2: SIS (Safety Instrumented System) | | Barrier 2: Emergency isolation |
| Barrier 3: Operator training | | Barrier 3: Evacuation plan |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Concept — "Barrier-Based Safety Management":** In Bow-Tie, the **"Swiss Cheese Model"** is implicit. When barriers are visualized as slices of Swiss cheese, the **holes represent failures**. An incident occurs when holes in all barriers align (Reason's Model, 1990).

---

## 2.3 Witness Interview Methodology (5-Step Protocol)

| Step | Action | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Preparation** | Review P&IDs, identify questions, choose quiet location |
| 2 | **Establish Rapport** | Explain purpose, ensure no blame, take notes |
| 3 | **Open-Ended Questions** | "What did you see/hear/notice?" |
| 4 | **Closed-Ended Verification** | "Was valve X open or closed?" |
| 5 | **Closure & Sign-Off** | Summarize, get witness signature, thank them |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Leading questions are **forbidden** ("Did you see that the valve was closed?" instead of "What was the valve's position?"). Examiners may deduct marks for this.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Quick Reference Table)

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Symbol | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **OR Gate Probability** | $P_{TE} = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$ | Fault Tree, parallel failures |
| **AND Gate Probability** | $P_{TE} = \prod P_i$ | Fault Tree, simultaneous failures |
| **ETA Outcome Probability** | $P_j = P_{init} \times \prod P_{k}$ | Event Tree, branch outcomes |
| **OSHA Injury Rate** | $IR = \frac{N \times 200{,}000}{H}$ | Incidence reporting |
| **Severity Rate** | $SR = \frac{L \times 200{,}000}{H}$ | Lost workday tracking |
| **DART Rate** | $DART = \frac{(D+R+T) \times 200{,}000}{H}$ | Days Away/Restricted/Transfer |
| **Near-Miss Ratio (Heinrich)** | $1 : 29 : 300$ | Major : Minor : Near-miss |
| **Reliability** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Component reliability over time |
| **Failure Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \frac{f}{N}$ | Failures per unit time per component |
| **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Mean Time Between Failures |
| **Bow-Tie Top Event** | Central node | Visualize causation and consequences |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Investigation Process is **not academic** — it directly impacts:

- **Refinery Operations** (BP Texas City 2005 — investigative failure led to 15 deaths).
- **Pharmaceutical Plant Safety** (cross-contamination root cause studies).
- **OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119** mandates a 5-year audit cycle **AND** incident investigation for all catastrophic events.
- **Insurance Premium Calculations** (insurers use incident rates and root cause closure rates to set premiums).
- **API RP 754** (Process Safety Performance Indicators) — Tier 1, 2, 3, 4 incident reporting is now a global standard.

---

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive FTA Calculation: Reactor Overpressure Scenario

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Problem:** A chemical reactor has **three parallel subsystems** that can fail and cause overpressure. Compute the Top Event (TE) probability for two fault tree configurations.

**Given Data:**

| Component | Failure Mode | Probability (per year) |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling Water Pump (CWP) | Bearing failure | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Temperature Sensor (TS) | Drift | $5 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) | Set-point failure | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Operator (OP) | Failure to intervene | $1 \times 10^{-2}$ |

---

### 3.1.1 Configuration 1: All Failures in OR Gate (Parallel, Any Failure Causes TE)

The Top Event occurs if **any one** subsystem fails.

$$P_{TE} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$P_{TE} = 1 - (1 - P_{CWP})(1 - P_{TS})(1 - P_{PRV})(1 - P_{OP})$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$P_{TE} = 1 - (1 - 0.001)(1 - 0.0005)(1 - 0.0001)(1 - 0.01)$$

Compute each factor:

$$(1 - 0.001) = 0.999$$

$$(1 - 0.0005) = 0.9995$$

$$(1 - 0.0001) = 0.9999$$

$$(1 - 0.01) = 0.99$$

Now multiply them sequentially:

$$0.999 \times 0.9995 = 0.9985005$$

$$0.9985005 \times 0.9999 = 0.99840065$$

$$0.99840065 \times 0.99 = 0.9884166435$$

Therefore:

$$P_{TE} = 1 - 0.9884166435 = 0.0115833565$$

$$P_{TE} \approx 1.158 \times 10^{-2} \text{ per year}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** With the OR-gate model, the **dominant contributor** is the **Operator failure (10⁻²)**, which is 10× higher than the next contributor. This signals that **human factors training** is the most impactful intervention.

---

### 3.1.2 Configuration 2: CWP AND TS Failures Cause TE (Common-Cause Subset)

Now suppose the Top Event occurs only if **CWP fails AND TS fails simultaneously** (sensor should have caught the temperature rise caused by pump failure).

$$P_{TE} = P_{CWP} \times P_{TS}$$

$$P_{TE} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per year}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This is a much lower probability, but the question becomes: **what if the CWP and TS share a common cause (e.g., both powered from the same UPS)?** This leads to **CCF (Common Cause Failure)** analysis, a deeper KTU topic.

---

### 3.1.3 Minimal Cut Set Identification

For Configuration 1 (OR gate), the **Minimal Cut Sets** are:
- MCS1: {CWP}
- MCS2: {TS}
- MCS3: {PRV}
- MCS4: {OP}

Each is **order 1** (single-point failures), so the system is vulnerable to any single failure. This is a **high-risk configuration** from a safety perspective.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive ETA Calculation: Compressed Gas Cylinder Rupture

> **Scenario:** A high-pressure gas cylinder ruptures. The probability of rupture is **$5 \times 10^{-4}$** per year. Three safety functions exist:
> - SF1: Bursting disc opens (P_success = 0.90)
> - SF2: Automatic shutoff valve (P_success = 0.85)
> - SF3: Vent to safe location (P_success = 0.80)

**Required:** Build the Event Tree and compute all four outcomes.

### Step 1: Define the Branching Structure

At each safety function, the branch is **Success (S)** or **Failure (F)**. With 3 safety functions, there are $2^3 = 8$ possible outcomes, but we collapse by **worst case** (if any function fails, severity increases).

### Step 2: Enumerate Outcomes

| Path | SF1 | SF2 | SF3 | Probability Calculation | P_outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | S | S | S | $0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.80$ | $0.6120$ |
| 2 | S | S | F | $0.90 \times 0.85 \times 0.20$ | $0.1530$ |
| 3 | S | F | S | $0.90 \times 0.15 \times 0.80$ | $0.1080$ |
| 4 | S | F | F | $0.90 \times 0.15 \times 0.20$ | $0.0270$ |
| 5 | F | S | S | $0.10 \times 0.85 \times 0.80$ | $0.0680$ |
| 6 | F | S | F | $0.10 \times 0.85 \times 0.20$ | $0.0170$ |
| 7 | F | F | S | $0.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.80$ | $0.0120$ |
| 8 | F | F | F | $0.10 \times 0.15 \times 0.20$ | $0.0030$ |

**Sum Verification:**

$$0.6120 + 0.1530 + 0.1080 + 0.0270 + 0.0680 + 0.0170 + 0.0120 + 0.0030 = 1.0000 \checkmark$$

### Step 3: Multiply by Initiating Event Probability

For each outcome $j$:

$$P_j = P_{init} \times P_{branch_j}$$

| Outcome | P_outcome (per rupture) | P_annual | Severity Tier |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.6120 | $3.06 \times 10^{-4}$ | Tier 4 (minor) |
| 2 | 0.1530 | $7.65 \times 10^{-5}$ | Tier 3 |
| 3 | 0.1080 | $5.40 \times 10^{-5}$ | Tier 3 |
| 4 | 0.0270 | $1.35 \times 10^{-5}$ | Tier 2 |
| 5 | 0.0680 | $3.40 \times 10^{-5}$ | Tier 2 |
| 6 | 0.0170 | $8.50 \times 10^{-6}$ | Tier 1 |
| 7 | 0.0120 | $6.00 \times 10^{-6}$ | Tier 1 |
| 8 | 0.0030 | $1.50 \times 10^{-6}$ | Tier 1 (catastrophic) |

### Step 4: Engineering Conclusion

The **single highest-probability outcome** ($P = 3.06 \times 10^{-4}$/year) is actually the **safest path** (all barriers work). However, the **cumulative Tier 1 catastrophic risk** is:

$$P_{Tier1} = (0.0170 + 0.0120 + 0.0030) \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.60 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per year}$$

This is the value that **risk matrices** use to determine ALARP (As Low As Reasonably Practicable) compliance.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Investigation Tool

| Investigation Need | Recommended Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Quick, single-cause incident | **5 Whys** | Fast, simple, requires no specialist software |
| Multi-failure complex system | **Fault Tree Analysis** | Boolean rigor, computes probability |
| Forward propagation of consequences | **Event Tree Analysis** | Maps all possible outcomes |
| Visual integration for management | **Bow-Tie Analysis** | One-page summary, intuitive |
| Categorical brainstorming | **Fishbone (Ishikawa)** | 6M categories: Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Milieu |
| Organizational / management failure | **MORT** | Tree structure with 100+ specific causes |
| Brainstorming-based screening | **SWIFT** | Lightweight, expert-driven |
| Behavioral analysis | **Human Factors Analysis and Classification System (HFACS)** | Layered human error model |

---

## 3.4 Worked-Out KTU Numerical: Heinrich's Pyramid

> **Question:** A plant has had **5 major incidents** in a year. Using Heinrich's Law (1:29:300), how many minor incidents and near-misses are expected?

### Step 1: Establish the Ratio

$$\text{Major : Minor : Near-miss} = 1 : 29 : 300$$

### Step 2: Compute Predicted Minor Incidents

Given Major = 5:

$$\text{Minor} = 5 \times 29 = 145$$

### Step 3: Compute Predicted Near-Misses

$$\text{Near-miss} = 5 \times 300 = 1500$$

### Step 4: Engineering Interpretation

The plant should have logged **145 minor** and **1500 near-misses** if Heinrich's ratio holds. If actual numbers are far below these, **the plant's near-miss reporting culture is weak**, and an investigation process itself is needed to improve it.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Heinrich's ratio is **empirical and historical** (1930s insurance data). Modern KTU 2024 answers should state that the ratio is **indicative, not exact**, and that Bird's updated ratio (1970s) is 1:10:30:600 (with property damage).

---

## 3.5 Reliability Function Derivation (Exponential Distribution)

For a non-repairable component with constant failure rate $\lambda$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Probability of failure within time t:**

$$F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Example:** A pressure transmitter has $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ per year. What is the reliability after 2 years?

$$R(2) = e^{-(5 \times 10^{-3})(2)} = e^{-0.01} = 0.99005 \approx 99.0\%$$

**Failure Probability:**

$$F(2) = 1 - 0.99005 = 0.00995 \approx 0.995\%$$

This is the **input data** an investigator would use when building an FTA.

# 📚 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Part A Pattern:** Each question carries 3 marks and is typically answered in 80–120 words.

---

### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Investigation Process" as applied to chemical process safety. List any four objectives of conducting an incident investigation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Investigation Process** in chemical process safety is a **systematic, structured methodology** used to determine the sequence, causes, and contributing factors of an undesired process event (incident, near-miss, or accident), with the goal of preventing recurrence.

**Four Key Objectives (½ mark each):**

1. **Determine the chronology** of events leading to the incident.
2. **Identify root and contributing causes** using analytical tools (5 Whys, FTA, Bow-Tie).
3. **Develop corrective and preventive actions** that are SMART and ranked by effectiveness.
4. **Disseminate lessons learned** to all stakeholders and update the Process Safety Management (PSM) system.

---

### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between a "Root Cause" and a "Contributing Cause" with one example each, in the context of an accident investigation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Root Cause | Contributing Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The fundamental, most basic reason that, if corrected, prevents recurrence. | A condition that increased the likelihood but did not directly cause the event. |
| **Depth** | Deepest, systemic level. | Intermediate, supporting level. |
| **Example** | Management's failure to enforce preventive maintenance schedules. | Vibration monitoring instrument was out of service during the incident. |

In a **pump-bearing failure** leading to reactor overpressure, the **root cause** is **inadequate maintenance culture**, while the **contributing cause** is **lack of online vibration monitoring**.

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Part B Pattern:** 14 marks, typically with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, with internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### 🔹 **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **A distillation column in a petrochemical plant experienced a sudden pressure rise, leading to a rupture of the safety relief valve and release of flammable vapors. There were no injuries but significant production loss.**

**(a) Design a step-by-step investigation process for this incident, clearly indicating the role of the investigation team. (7 marks)**

**(b) Construct a Bow-Tie diagram for this scenario, identifying at least two threats, the top event, two consequences, and corresponding preventive and mitigative barriers. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Initial Response and Scene Control (1 mark)**
- Activate the Emergency Operations Plan (EOP).
- Evacuate non-essential personnel from a radius of **at least 1.5 × vessel height**.
- Isolate fuel/heat sources and apply Lock-Out/Tag-Out (LOTO).
- Designate an Incident Commander and start a timestamped logbook.
- Render first aid; call emergency responders.

**Step 2: Formation of Investigation Team (1 mark)**
The team should be **multidisciplinary**:
- Team Leader (Senior Process Engineer)
- Safety Officer
- Maintenance Engineer
- Operations Supervisor
- Human Factors Specialist
- Legal/Compliance Officer
- External Expert (if required)

**Step 3: Evidence Collection and Preservation (2 marks)**
- **Physical:** Failed relief valve (chain of custody), distillation column internals, residue samples (refrigerated < 4°C), pressure chart recordings.
- **Documentary:** P&ID, SOP, shift logs, maintenance records, DCS historian data (trends, alarms).
- **Witness:** Operators on duty, maintenance crew, supervisors — using **5-step interview protocol** (open-ended questions, no leading).
- **Environmental:** Wind speed, temperature, drainage pattern, plant layout.

**Step 4: Cause Analysis (2 marks)**
- Construct a **chronology** of events.
- Apply **5 Whys** to drill down from the symptom.
- Build a **Fault Tree** to identify all failure paths.
- Develop a **Bow-Tie** (see part b).
- Verify findings against DCS data, witness statements, and physical evidence.

**Step 5: Corrective Actions and Reporting (1 mark)**
- SMART recommendations ranked by hierarchy (Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE).
- Write the report with all 9 KTU sections.
- Distribute to operations, maintenance, engineering, safety, management, regulators.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Bow-Tie Diagram for Distillation Column Overpressure:**

| Element | Content |
|---|---|
| **Threat 1** | Cooling water failure (pump trip, valve closure) |
| **Threat 2** | Reflux pump failure (loss of condensate return) |
| **Top Event** | Distillation column overpressure |
| **Consequence 1** | Flammable vapor cloud release |
| **Consequence 2** | Column structural damage / explosion |

**Preventive Barriers (Left Side):**
1. High-pressure alarm with operator intervention
2. Independent Safety Instrumented System (SIS) — automatic column shutdown
3. Preventive maintenance program for reflux and cooling pumps

**Mitigative Barriers (Right Side):**
1. Pressure relief valve (PSV) — primary mitigation
2. Rupture disc (secondary, in case PSV fails)
3. Emergency vent system to flare
4. Gas detection system with automatic isolation
5. Emergency evacuation plan and firewater deluge

> **Graphical representation (verbal description for the answer sheet):** Draw a central node labeled "Top Event: Distillation Column Overpressure." On the left, draw two arrows (Threat 1, Threat 2) leading into the Top Event, with three horizontal barrier symbols blocking them. On the right, draw two arrows (Consequence 1, Consequence 2) leading out of the Top Event, with three horizontal barrier symbols blocking them. Label each barrier clearly.

---

### 🔹 **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **A pharmaceutical reactor experienced a runaway exothermic reaction, leading to a vessel rupture and release of toxic intermediates. The control system had logged multiple alarms in the 30 minutes before the event.**

**(a) Explain how you would perform a Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) for this incident, including a worked-out numerical example. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the various root cause analysis tools available to the investigator, comparing their advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Define the Top Event (1 mark)**

The Top Event (TE) is: **"Reactor runaway reaction leading to rupture."**

**Step 2: Identify Intermediate Events (1 mark)**

For an exothermic runaway, two intermediate causes are typically modeled:
- IE1: Failure of cooling system
- IE2: Failure of temperature control
- IE3: Incorrect reactant charge

The Top Event occurs if **IE1 OR IE2 OR IE3** occurs.

**Step 3: Identify Basic Events (1 mark)**

- BE1: Cooling water pump fails ($\lambda = 2 \times 10^{-3}$/yr)
- BE2: Cooling water valve closed (Human Error) ($P = 1 \times 10^{-2}$/yr)
- BE3: Temperature sensor drifts ($P = 5 \times 10^{-4}$/yr)
- BE4: Temperature controller fails ($P = 3 \times 10^{-4}$/yr)
- BE5: Operator misreads charge ($\lambda = 1 \times 10^{-2}$/yr)
- BE6: Charge pump overfeeds ($P = 1 \times 10^{-3}$/yr)

**Step 4: Construct the Boolean Equations (1 mark)**

$$TE = IE1 \cup IE2 \cup IE3$$

$$IE1 = BE1 \cup BE2$$

$$IE2 = BE3 \cap BE4$$

$$IE3 = BE5 \cup BE6$$

**Step 5: Compute Top Event Probability (2 marks)**

**IE1 (OR gate):**

$$P_{IE1} = 1 - (1 - 0.002)(1 - 0.01) = 1 - (0.998)(0.99) = 1 - 0.98802 = 0.01198$$

**IE2 (AND gate):**

$$P_{IE2} = 0.0005 \times 0.0003 = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$$

**IE3 (OR gate):**

$$P_{IE3} = 1 - (1 - 0.01)(1 - 0.001) = 1 - (0.99)(0.999) = 1 - 0.98901 = 0.01099$$

**TE (OR gate of IE1, IE2, IE3):**

$$P_{TE} = 1 - (1 - 0.01198)(1 - 1.5 \times 10^{-7})(1 - 0.01099)$$

$$= 1 - (0.98802)(0.99999985)(0.98901)$$

$$= 1 - 0.97706 \approx 0.02294 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 6: Engineering Interpretation (1 mark)**

The **dominant contributors** are the **human error events (BE2 and BE5)**, both at $10^{-2}$/yr. This indicates that **operator training and procedure clarity** are the highest-impact interventions — far more effective than replacing instruments.

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Comparison of Root Cause Analysis Tools (Table format — 7 marks allocated as 1 mark per well-explained tool, with comparative remarks):**

| Tool | Best Use | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **5 Whys** | Simple, single-path incidents | Fast, intuitive, no specialist tools | Assumes linear causation; misses multi-path failures |
| **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Complex, multi-failure systems | Boolean rigor; computes probability; identifies minimal cut sets | Requires accurate failure data; can be time-consuming |
| **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** | Forward propagation of outcomes | Maps all outcomes; gives probabilities; aligns with emergency planning | Requires well-defined safety functions and probabilities |
| **Bow-Tie Analysis** | Integrated visualization | Combines FTA + ETA; intuitive for management | Less quantitative; barrier effectiveness not easily ranked |
| **Fishbone (Ishikawa)** | Brainstorming and categorization | Multi-dimensional (6Ms); encourages team participation | Subjective; no probabilistic output |
| **SWIFT** | Early-stage risk screening | Lightweight, expert-driven; quick | Highly dependent on expert quality |
| **MORT** | Management/systemic failures | Comprehensive 100+ cause taxonomy; identifies organizational gaps | Complex; can be overwhelming; rarely used in industry |
| **HFACS** | Human error analysis | Layered (4 levels: unsafe acts, precondition, supervision, organizational) | Limited to human factors only |

**Comparative Summary (KTU 2024 Expectation):**
- For **quick investigations** → 5 Whys.
- For **complex PSM-level analysis** → FTA + Bow-Tie.
- For **organizational learning** → MORT.
- A **complete investigation** typically uses **at least 2 tools** for triangulation.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU 2024 Investigation Process Questions:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the "Initial Response" stage** — Many students jump directly to root cause analysis. Examiners expect explicit mention of EOP activation, scene securing, and LOTO. **Penalty: up to 2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Conflating "Cause" and "Root Cause"** — A common error is treating the immediate cause (e.g., "valve failed") as the root cause. The root cause is **systemic** (e.g., "no preventive replacement program"). Examiners explicitly look for this distinction. **Penalty: up to 2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Omitting "Near-Miss Investigation"** — KTU 2024 places heavy emphasis on **proactive** investigation. Students who only discuss post-incident investigation may miss 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Chain of Custody** — In evidence collection questions, forgetting to mention documentation/preservation of physical evidence. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Incorrect FTA Boolean Logic** — Mixing up OR and AND gates is a **fatal error**. Always state the gate type explicitly.
>
> 6. **Not Drawing the Bow-Tie** — Many KTU 2024 questions ask for a Bow-Tie. A verbal description is acceptable, but **must include all 4 elements**: Threats, Top Event, Consequences, Barriers.
>
> 7. **Ignoring Hierarchy of Controls** — When listing corrective actions, students often present PPE-level solutions first. Examiners want **Elimination/Substitution first**.

---

## 5.4 ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4: Investigation Process**

- [ ] **Definition:** Investigation is a **systematic, blame-free, multidisciplinary** process to find root causes and prevent recurrence.
- [ ] **Five Stages:** Initial Response → Evidence Collection → Cause Analysis → Corrective Actions → Reporting & Follow-up.
- [ ] **Initial Response Time Window:** T+0 to T+2 hours is critical for evidence preservation.
- [ ] **Evacuation Radius:** Typically **1.5 × height of the tallest vessel**.
- [ ] **Four Evidence Classes:** Physical, Documentary, Witness, Environmental.
- [ ] **Chain of Custody** must be maintained for all physical evidence.
- [ ] **5 Whys:** Linear tool, **best for simple incidents**, but limited for multi-path causation.
- [ ] **FTA Symbols:** Rectangle = Event, Circle = Basic Event, OR Gate = Union, AND Gate = Intersection.
- [ ] **FTA OR Gate Formula:** $P_{TE} = 1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$
- [ ] **FTA AND Gate Formula:** $P_{TE} = \prod P_i$
- [ ] **ETA:** Forward propagation; outcome probability = $P_{init} \times \prod P_{function}$.
- [ ] **Bow-Tie:** Combines FTA (left) + ETA (right); central node = Top Event.
- [ ] **Swiss Cheese Model (Reason):** Incidents occur when holes in multiple barriers align.
- [ ] **SMART Corrective Actions:** Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- [ ] **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE.
- [ ] **Heinrich's Pyramid:** 1 : 29 : 300 (Major : Minor : Near-miss).
- [ ] **OSHA Rates:** Use **200,000 hours** as denominator (100 FTE × 2000 hrs).
- [ ] **Report Sections:** Executive Summary → Description → Sequence → Causes → Recommendations → Implementation → Lessons Learned.
- [ ] **Report Distribution:** Operations, Maintenance, Engineering, Safety, Management, Regulators, Trade Unions.
- [ ] **Investigation Team:** Multidisciplinary — Safety, Process, Maintenance, Operations, Human Factors, Legal, Environmental.
- [ ] **Interview Protocol:** 5 steps — Prepare, Rapport, Open-ended, Closed verification, Closure.
- [ ] **Avoid Leading Questions** in witness interviews.
- [ ] **Reliability Function:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ (exponential distribution).
- [ ] **MTBF** = $1/\lambda$ (for constant failure rate).
- [ ] **CCPS & API RP 754** are the governing standards for process incident investigation.
- [ ] **PSM Cycle** includes investigation as a feedback loop for continuous improvement.
- [ ] **Regulatory Hooks:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119, EPA RMP, Indian Factories Act.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | Course: OESFT616 — Chemical Process Safety**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OESFT616 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Investigation Summary

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Investigation Summary** in Chemical Process Safety refers to the structured, formal compilation of findings, conclusions, root causes, and corrective actions derived from a systematic incident investigation. It serves as the authoritative closing document that translates raw investigative data into actionable safety intelligence for the organization, regulatory bodies, and the broader process safety community.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The *Investigation Summary* is the concluding phase of the incident investigation methodology wherein the investigation team consolidates the sequence of events, identifies underlying root causes (technical, human, and organizational), formulates corrective and preventive recommendations, and disseminates the lessons learned to prevent recurrence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an **Investigation Summary** like a doctor's discharge summary after a patient has been treated in a hospital:

| Hospital Analogy | Investigation Summary Equivalent |
|------------------|----------------------------------|
| Patient's symptoms recorded | Incident facts and sequence of events |
| Diagnostic tests performed | Root cause analysis (fault tree, 5-Why) |
| Final diagnosis | Underlying root causes identified |
| Prescribed medication | Corrective actions and recommendations |
| Discharge instructions | Lessons learned and follow-up plan |
| Sent to family doctor | Disseminated to management/regulators |

Just as a discharge summary ensures continuity of care, an **Investigation Summary** ensures continuity of safety — it transfers the knowledge gained from a single unfortunate event into systemic improvements that protect the entire organization.

## 1.3 Purpose and Objectives

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Objectives of an Investigation Summary:**
> 1. **Document** the factual chain of events leading to the incident
> 2. **Identify** immediate, contributing, and root causes
> 3. **Recommend** engineering, administrative, and procedural controls
> 4. **Communicate** findings to stakeholders, regulators, and industry
> 5. **Prevent** recurrence through systemic learning
> 6. **Comply** with OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119), EPA RMP, and KTU-recommended safety standards

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

- **Reporting Time:** Initial summary within **48 hours** of incident
- **Full Report Time:** Complete summary within **30 days** for major incidents
- **Distribution Lead Time:** Within **7 days** post-approval
- **Follow-up Review:** Effectiveness audit at **90, 180, and 365 days**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Investigation Summary Architecture

The investigation summary is organized into a hierarchical structure that mirrors the **Swiss Cheese Model** of accident causation, ensuring that every layer of failure is addressed.

### 2.1.1 Primary Components (Mandatory)

1. **Executive Summary** — One-page synopsis for senior management
2. **Incident Description** — What, where, when, who, and how
3. **Sequence of Events** — Chronological timeline with critical decision points
4. **Evidence Analysis** — Physical, documentary, and witness evidence
5. **Causal Analysis** — Root causes using formal methodologies
6. **Recommendations** — Engineering, procedural, and training controls
7. **Implementation Plan** — Responsible parties, deadlines, resources
8. **Lessons Learned** — Generalized knowledge for industry dissemination
9. **Appendices** — Supporting data, photographs, calculations, references

### 2.1.2 The Causal Layer Hierarchy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Layers of Causes (per KTU/CCPS Guidelines):**
> 1. **Primary Cause** — The immediate unsafe act or condition
> 2. **Secondary Cause** — The enabling circumstances
> 3. **Contributing Cause** — Management system failures
> 4. **Root Cause** — Fundamental organizational or design deficiency
> 5. **Underlying Cause** — Cultural, economic, or regulatory factors

## 2.2 Summary Quality Evaluation Framework

The quality of an investigation summary is assessed using the **SMART Criteria** applied to each recommendation:

| Criterion | Meaning | Example |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **S** | Specific | "Install a pressure relief valve on reactor R-201" |
| **M** | Measurable | "Reduce deviation frequency to <1 per year" |
| **A** | Achievable | Feasible within current budget and technology |
| **R** | Relevant | Directly addresses identified root cause |
| **T** | Time-bound | Completion within 90 days of report approval |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Application |
|---------------|------------|-------------|
| $N_{inc}$ | Number of incidents in period | Trend analysis |
| $\lambda$ | Incident rate per unit time | Statistical baseline |
| $t_{r}$ | Time to report (days) | Compliance metric |
| $C_{p}$ | Cost of prevention (\$) | Investment metric |
| $C_{f}$ | Cost of failure (\$) | Justification metric |
| $R_{ROI}$ | Return on Safety Investment | $R_{ROI} = \frac{C_{f} - C_{p}}{C_{p}} \times 100\%$ |
| $P_{rec}$ | Probability of recurrence | Risk re-assessment |
| $S_{eff}$ | Summary effectiveness score | 0–100 scale |
| $f_{close}$ | Closure fraction of recommendations | $\frac{N_{closed}}{N_{total}}$ |

### 2.3.1 Key Risk Re-Assessment Equation

The residual risk after implementation of recommendations is computed as:

$$R_{residual} = P_{rec} \times C_{severity} \times F_{exposure}$$

where:
- $P_{rec}$ = Probability of recurrence after corrective action
- $C_{severity}$ = Consequence severity (typically 1–5 on CCPS scale)
- $F_{exposure}$ = Frequency of exposure to the hazard (per year)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The investigation summary framework is applied across:
- **Oil & Gas:** Post-Blowout reports (Deepwater Horizon, Piper Alpha)
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Reactor thermal runaway investigations
- **Nuclear:** NUREG-style root cause reports
- **Chemical Manufacturing:** CSB (US Chemical Safety Board) case closures
- **KTU Industrial Visits:** Incident case studies in process plant internships
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Recurrence Reduction Effectiveness Index (RREI)

The effectiveness of an investigation summary in preventing recurrence can be quantified. Let us derive the **RREI** from first principles.

### Step 1: Define Baseline Incident Frequency

Let $\lambda_{0}$ be the baseline incident rate (incidents per year) before the investigation and corrective actions.

$$\lambda_{0} = \frac{N_{inc,0}}{T_{0}}$$

where $N_{inc,0}$ is the number of incidents in the baseline period $T_{0}$ (typically 3 years).

### Step 2: Define Post-Correction Incident Frequency

Let $\lambda_{1}$ be the observed incident rate after recommendations are implemented.

$$\lambda_{1} = \frac{N_{inc,1}}{T_{1}}$$

### Step 3: Define the Reduction Ratio

The fractional reduction in incident rate is:

$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{\lambda_{0} - \lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{0}}$$

### Step 4: Apply Quality Weighting Factor

Not all summaries are equally effective. Define a quality weighting factor $w$ based on the SMART criteria compliance:

$$w = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} S_{i}$$

where $S_{i} \in [0,1]$ is the SMART score for the $i$-th recommendation (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).

### Step 5: Final RREI Formula

$$\boxed{\text{RREI} = w \times \Delta\lambda \times 100\%}$$

A higher RREI indicates a more effective investigation summary.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Closure Effectiveness Metric (CEM)

The **Closure Effectiveness Metric** quantifies how well an organization follows through on its own investigation recommendations.

### Step 1: Count Total Recommendations

$$N_{total} = N_{eng} + N_{proc} + N_{train}$$

where $N_{eng}$, $N_{proc}$, and $N_{train}$ are engineering, procedural, and training recommendations respectively.

### Step 2: Count Closed Recommendations

$$N_{closed} = N_{closed,eng} + N_{closed,proc} + N_{closed,train}$$

### Step 3: Compute Closure Fraction

$$f_{close} = \frac{N_{closed}}{N_{total}}$$

### Step 4: Compute Time-Weighted Closure (more rigorous)

$$f_{close}^{TW} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{closed}} \frac{1}{t_{i,actual} / t_{i,target}}}{N_{total}}$$

This penalizes late closures. The final CEM is:

$$\boxed{\text{CEM} = f_{close}^{TW} \times 100\%}$$

A CEM of **100%** means all recommendations were closed on or before their target dates.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A chemical plant experienced a hydrocarbon release from a flange on reactor R-301. The investigation produced 12 recommendations (5 engineering, 4 procedural, 3 training). After 6 months, 9 were closed, of which 6 were on time. The SMART scores averaged 0.85, and the baseline incident rate of 2.5/year dropped to 0.8/year over a 2-year post-incident period. Calculate the RREI and CEM (assume the 6-month on-time closures were within target).

### Solution — Part (a): RREI Calculation

Step 1: Baseline rate.
$$\lambda_{0} = 2.5 \text{ per year}$$

Step 2: Post-correction rate.
$$\lambda_{1} = 0.8 \text{ per year}$$

Step 3: Reduction ratio.
$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{2.5 - 0.8}{2.5} = \frac{1.7}{2.5} = 0.68$$

Step 4: Quality factor.
$$w = 0.85$$

Step 5: RREI.
$$\text{RREI} = 0.85 \times 0.68 \times 100\% = 57.8\%$$

### Solution — Part (b): CEM Calculation

Step 1: Total recommendations.
$$N_{total} = 12$$

Step 2: Closed recommendations.
$$N_{closed} = 9$$

Step 3: Closure fraction.
$$f_{close} = \frac{9}{12} = 0.75$$

Step 4: Time-weighted factor (6 on-time, 3 late → use 1.0 for on-time, 0.5 for late average).
$$f_{close}^{TW} = \frac{6 \times 1.0 + 3 \times 0.5}{12} = \frac{7.5}{12} = 0.625$$

Step 5: CEM.
$$\text{CEM} = 0.625 \times 100\% = 62.5\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The investigation summary reduced recurrence risk by approximately 58% (good) but only 62.5% of recommendations were properly closed, indicating a follow-up gap that management should address.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Summary Approval

| Summary Quality Indicator | Excellent | Acceptable | Poor |
|---------------------------|-----------|------------|------|
| Executive Summary clarity | 1-page, no jargon | 2-pages, some jargon | >3 pages, confusing |
| Root cause depth | 5-Why + Fault Tree | 5-Why only | Single cause stated |
| Recommendations SMART | 100% SMART | 75% SMART | <50% SMART |
| Evidence trail | Complete & signed | Mostly complete | Gaps present |
| Closure tracking | Real-time dashboard | Monthly review | No tracking |
| RREI achieved | >70% | 40–70% | <40% |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Investigation Summary Workflow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Occurs] --> B[Emergency Response]
    B --> C[Scene Preservation]
    C --> D[Evidence Collection]
    D --> E[Witness Interviews]
    E --> F[Data Analysis]
    F --> G[Root Cause Identification]
    G --> H[Recommendations Drafting]
    H --> I[Executive Summary Writing]
    I --> J[Peer Review]
    J --> K{Approved?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Final Report Issued]
    K -->|No| M[Revise and Resubmit]
    M --> J
    L --> N[Distribution to Stakeholders]
    N --> O[Implementation of Actions]
    O --> P[90-Day Review]
    P --> Q[180-Day Review]
    Q --> R[365-Day Audit]
    R --> S[Lessons Learned Database]
    S --> T[Industry Dissemination]
```

## 4.2 Investigation Summary Document Structure (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DOC[Investigation Summary Document] --> S1[Section 1: Executive Summary]
    DOC --> S2[Section 2: Incident Overview]
    DOC --> S3[Section 3: Sequence of Events]
    DOC --> S4[Section 4: Evidence Analysis]
    DOC --> S5[Section 5: Causal Analysis]
    DOC --> S6[Section 6: Root Cause Findings]
    DOC --> S7[Section 7: Recommendations]
    DOC --> S8[Section 8: Implementation Plan]
    DOC --> S9[Section 9: Lessons Learned]
    DOC --> S10[Section 10: Appendices]

    S5 --> S5A[Immediate Cause]
    S5 --> S5B[Contributing Cause]
    S5 --> S5C[Root Cause]
    S5 --> S5D[Systemic Cause]

    S7 --> S7A[Engineering Controls]
    S7 --> S7B[Administrative Controls]
    S7 --> S7C[PPE Controls]
    S7 --> S7D[Training Actions]
```

## 4.3 Causal Layer Hierarchy (Swiss Cheese Model Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Unsafe Act / Condition] --> L2[Precursor Event]
    L2 --> L3[Loss of Barrier]
    L3 --> L4[Management System Failure]
    L4 --> L5[Organizational Culture]
    L5 --> L6[Regulatory / Economic Environment]
    L6 --> L7[Investigation Summary Target]
```

## 4.4 Recommendation Closure Tracking Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    REC[Recommendation Issued] --> ASS[Responsible Person Assigned]
    ASS --> BUD[Budget Allocated]
    BUD --> PLAN[Action Plan Created]
    PLAN --> EXEC[Execution in Progress]
    EXEC --> VER[Verification by Safety Officer]
    VER --> EFF[Effectiveness Check]
    EFF --> CL[Closed in Database]
    EFF --> NC[Not Effective - Reopen]
    NC --> EXEC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `REC`) and contain no reserved keywords, markdown formatting, or HTML tags. All labels are plain uppercase text.
